title: 2015 NFL season

infobox.value: [Levi's Stadium](/wiki/Levi%27s_Stadium "Levi's Stadium"), [Santa Clara, California](/wiki/Santa_Clara,_California "Santa Clara, California")

was the 96th season in the history of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL), and the 50th in the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") era. To celebrate the 50th season of the Super Bowl, a gold-plated NFL logo and other various gold-themed promotions were used throughout the season. It began on Thursday, September 10, 2015, with the [annual kickoff game](/wiki/National_Football_League_Kickoff_game "National Football League Kickoff game") featuring the defending [Super Bowl XLIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIX "Super Bowl XLIX") champion [New England Patriots](/wiki/2015_New_England_Patriots "2015 New England Patriots") defeating the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/2015_Pittsburgh_Steelers_season "2015 Pittsburgh Steelers season"). The season concluded with [Super Bowl 50](/wiki/Super_Bowl_50 "Super Bowl 50"), the league's [championship game](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl"), on Sunday, February 7, 2016, at [Levi's Stadium](/wiki/Levi%27s_Stadium "Levi's Stadium") in [Santa Clara, California](/wiki/Santa_Clara,_California "Santa Clara, California"),[[2]](#cite_note-ESPN20140604-2) with the [Denver Broncos](/wiki/2015_Denver_Broncos_season "2015 Denver Broncos season") defeating the [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/2015_Carolina_Panthers_season "2015 Carolina Panthers season") 24–10.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American football season

This article is about the American football season in the United States. For the Gaelic football season in Ireland, see [2015 National Football League (Ireland)](/wiki/2015_National_Football_League_(Ireland) "2015 National Football League (Ireland)").

2015 NFL season

| * ← [2014](/wiki/2014_NFL_season "2014 NFL season") * **[NFL seasons](/wiki/List_of_NFL_seasons "List of NFL seasons")** * [2016](/wiki/2016_NFL_season "2016 NFL season") → | |

The **2015 NFL season** was the 96th season in the history of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL), and the 50th in the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") era. To celebrate the 50th season of the Super Bowl, a gold-plated NFL logo and other various gold-themed promotions were used throughout the season. It began on Thursday, September 10, 2015, with the [annual kickoff game](/wiki/National_Football_League_Kickoff_game "National Football League Kickoff game") featuring the defending [Super Bowl XLIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIX "Super Bowl XLIX") champion [New England Patriots](/wiki/2015_New_England_Patriots "2015 New England Patriots") defeating the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/2015_Pittsburgh_Steelers_season "2015 Pittsburgh Steelers season"). The season concluded with [Super Bowl 50](/wiki/Super_Bowl_50 "Super Bowl 50"), the league's [championship game](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl"), on Sunday, February 7, 2016, at [Levi's Stadium](/wiki/Levi%27s_Stadium "Levi's Stadium") in [Santa Clara, California](/wiki/Santa_Clara,_California "Santa Clara, California"),[[2]](#cite_note-ESPN20140604-2) with the [Denver Broncos](/wiki/2015_Denver_Broncos_season "2015 Denver Broncos season") defeating the [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/2015_Carolina_Panthers_season "2015 Carolina Panthers season") 24–10.

During the 2015 season, the [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders "Oakland Raiders"), the [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams"), and the [San Diego Chargers](/wiki/San_Diego_Chargers "San Diego Chargers") announced their intentions to relocate back to [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") in the ensuing off-season (all three teams had previously resided in the city at various points in their history). NFL owners eventually only approved the relocation of the Rams, by a vote of 30–2 on January 12, 2016. Thus, 2015 ended up being the Rams' last season in [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis").

## Player movement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Player movement")]

The 2015 NFL League Year began on Tuesday, March 10, 2015, at 4:00 p.m. ET. On Saturday, March 7, clubs started to contact and enter into contract negotiations with the certified agents of players who became unrestricted free agents upon the expiration of their [2014](/wiki/2014_NFL_season "2014 NFL season") contracts at 4:00 p.m. ET on March 10.[[3]](#cite_note-3) On Tuesday, March 10, 2015, clubs exercised options for 2015 on all players who have option clauses in their 2014 contracts, submitted qualifying offers to their restricted free agents with expiring contracts and to whom they desire to retain a Right of First Refusal/Compensation, submitted a Minimum Salary Tender to retain exclusive negotiating rights to their players with expiring 2014 contracts and who have fewer than three accrued seasons of free agency credit, "Top-51" began, all clubs must be under the 2015 salary cap, all 2014 player contracts expired at 4:00 p.m. ET and trading period for 2015 began. (4:00 p.m. ET).[[4]](#cite_note-4)

This season's salary cap increased to $143.28 million per team, up from $133 million in 2014.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Free agency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Free agency")]

A total of 453 players were eligible for some form of free agency at the beginning of the free agency period.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Among the players who changed teams via free agency included:

* Quarterbacks [Brian Hoyer](/wiki/Brian_Hoyer "Brian Hoyer") (Cleveland to Houston) and [Josh McCown](/wiki/Josh_McCown "Josh McCown") (Tampa Bay to Cleveland).
* Running backs [Frank Gore](/wiki/Frank_Gore "Frank Gore") (from San Francisco to Indianapolis),[[7]](#cite_note-7) [Chris Johnson](/wiki/Chris_Johnson_(running_back) "Chris Johnson (running back)") (from New York Jets to Arizona),[[8]](#cite_note-8) [Ryan Mathews](/wiki/Ryan_Mathews_(American_football) "Ryan Mathews (American football)") (from San Diego to Philadelphia),[[9]](#cite_note-9) [DeMarco Murray](/wiki/DeMarco_Murray "DeMarco Murray") (from Dallas to Philadelphia),[[10]](#cite_note-10) and [Shane Vereen](/wiki/Shane_Vereen "Shane Vereen") (New England to New York Giants)
* Wide receivers [Dwayne Bowe](/wiki/Dwayne_Bowe "Dwayne Bowe") (Kansas City to Cleveland), [Michael Crabtree](/wiki/Michael_Crabtree "Michael Crabtree"), [Andre Johnson](/wiki/Andre_Johnson "Andre Johnson") (Houston to Indianapolis),[[11]](#cite_note-11) [Jeremy Maclin](/wiki/Jeremy_Maclin "Jeremy Maclin") (Philadelphia to Kansas City),[[12]](#cite_note-12) [Eddie Royal](/wiki/Eddie_Royal "Eddie Royal") (San Diego to Chicago),[[13]](#cite_note-13) and [Torrey Smith](/wiki/Torrey_Smith "Torrey Smith") (Baltimore to San Francisco),[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* Tight ends [Jordan Cameron](/wiki/Jordan_Cameron "Jordan Cameron") (Cleveland to Miami), [Charles Clay](/wiki/Charles_Clay_(American_football) "Charles Clay (American football)") (Miami to Buffalo), [Owen Daniels](/wiki/Owen_Daniels_(American_football) "Owen Daniels (American football)") (Baltimore to Denver), [Lee Smith](/wiki/Lee_Smith_(American_football) "Lee Smith (American football)") (Buffalo to Oakland) and [Julius Thomas](/wiki/Julius_Thomas "Julius Thomas") (Denver to Jacksonville)
* Offensive linemen [James Carpenter](/wiki/James_Carpenter_(offensive_lineman) "James Carpenter (offensive lineman)") (Seattle to New York Jets),[[15]](#cite_note-15) [Orlando Franklin](/wiki/Orlando_Franklin "Orlando Franklin") (Denver to San Diego),[[16]](#cite_note-16) [Rodney Hudson](/wiki/Rodney_Hudson "Rodney Hudson") (Kansas City to Oakland),[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) [Mike Iupati](/wiki/Mike_Iupati "Mike Iupati") (San Francisco to Arizona),[[19]](#cite_note-19) [Jermey Parnell](/wiki/Jermey_Parnell "Jermey Parnell") (Dallas to Jacksonville) and [Stefen Wisniewski](/wiki/Stefen_Wisniewski "Stefen Wisniewski") (Oakland to Jacksonville)
* Defensive tackles [Nick Fairley](/wiki/Nick_Fairley "Nick Fairley") (from Detroit to St. Louis),[[20]](#cite_note-20) [Terrance Knighton](/wiki/Terrance_Knighton "Terrance Knighton") (from Denver to Washington),[[21]](#cite_note-21) [Jared Odrick](/wiki/Jared_Odrick "Jared Odrick") (Miami to Jacksonville, [Stephen Paea](/wiki/Stephen_Paea "Stephen Paea") (Chicago to Washington), [Ndamukong Suh](/wiki/Ndamukong_Suh "Ndamukong Suh") (Detroit to Miami),[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) [Vince Wilfork](/wiki/Vince_Wilfork "Vince Wilfork") (New England to Houston);[[24]](#cite_note-24) and [Dan Williams](/wiki/Dan_Williams_(defensive_tackle) "Dan Williams (defensive tackle)") (Arizona to Oakland)[[25]](#cite_note-25)
* Defensive ends [Greg Hardy](/wiki/Greg_Hardy "Greg Hardy") (Carolina to Dallas),[[26]](#cite_note-26) [Michael Johnson](/wiki/Michael_Johnson_(defensive_end) "Michael Johnson (defensive end)") (Tampa Bay to Cincinnati), [Pernell McPhee](/wiki/Pernell_McPhee "Pernell McPhee") (Baltimore to Chicago), [Jabaal Sheard](/wiki/Jabaal_Sheard "Jabaal Sheard") (Cleveland to New England) and [Brian Orakpo](/wiki/Brian_Orakpo "Brian Orakpo") (Washington to Tennessee),[[27]](#cite_note-27)
* Linebackers [Bruce Carter](/wiki/Bruce_Carter_(American_football) "Bruce Carter (American football)") (Dallas to Tampa Bay), [Nate Irving](/wiki/Nate_Irving "Nate Irving") (Denver to Indianapolis).,[[28]](#cite_note-28) [Brooks Reed](/wiki/Brooks_Reed "Brooks Reed") (Houston to Atlanta) and [Sean Weatherspoon](/wiki/Sean_Weatherspoon "Sean Weatherspoon") (Atlanta to Arizona)
* Cornerbacks [Perrish Cox](/wiki/Perrish_Cox "Perrish Cox") (San Francisco to Tennessee), [Antonio Cromartie](/wiki/Antonio_Cromartie "Antonio Cromartie") (Arizona to New York Jets),[[29]](#cite_note-29) [Chris Culliver](/wiki/Chris_Culliver "Chris Culliver") (San Francisco to Washington), [Byron Maxwell](/wiki/Byron_Maxwell "Byron Maxwell") (Seattle to Philadelphia),[[30]](#cite_note-30) [Darrelle Revis](/wiki/Darrelle_Revis "Darrelle Revis") (New England to New York Jets),[[31]](#cite_note-31) [Buster Skrine](/wiki/Buster_Skrine "Buster Skrine") (Cleveland to New York Jets), [Cary Williams](/wiki/Cary_Williams "Cary Williams") (Philadelphia to Seattle) and [Tramon Williams](/wiki/Tramon_Williams "Tramon Williams") (Green Bay to Cleveland)[[32]](#cite_note-32)
* Safeties [Rahim Moore](/wiki/Rahim_Moore "Rahim Moore") (Denver to Houston), [Antrel Rolle](/wiki/Antrel_Rolle "Antrel Rolle") (New York Giants to Chicago) and [Da'Norris Searcy](/wiki/Da%27Norris_Searcy "Da'Norris Searcy") (Buffalo to Tennessee)

Four players were assigned the [non-exclusive franchise tag](/wiki/Franchise_tag#Types_of_franchise_tag "Franchise tag") by their teams, which ensured that the team would receive compensation were the player to sign a contract with another team. These players were wide receivers [Dez Bryant](/wiki/Dez_Bryant "Dez Bryant") ([Cowboys](/wiki/2015_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2015 Dallas Cowboys season"))[[33]](#cite_note-33) and [Demaryius Thomas](/wiki/Demaryius_Thomas "Demaryius Thomas") ([Broncos](/wiki/2015_Denver_Broncos_season "2015 Denver Broncos season")),[[34]](#cite_note-34) linebacker [Justin Houston](/wiki/Justin_Houston "Justin Houston") ([Chiefs](/wiki/2015_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2015 Kansas City Chiefs season")),[[35]](#cite_note-35) and defensive end [Jason Pierre-Paul](/wiki/Jason_Pierre-Paul "Jason Pierre-Paul") ([Giants](/wiki/2015_New_York_Giants_season "2015 New York Giants season")).[[36]](#cite_note-36) One other team used the [transition tag](/wiki/Franchise_tag#Types_of_franchise_tag "Franchise tag"), which offers the player's current team a chance to match offers from other franchises and also guarantees draft pick compensation (at a lesser level than the franchise tag) if a tagged player signs elsewhere. The player given the transition tag was [Charles Clay](/wiki/Charles_Clay_(American_football) "Charles Clay (American football)") ([Dolphins](/wiki/2015_Miami_Dolphins_season "2015 Miami Dolphins season")). On March 19, 2015, Clay signed a five-year, $38M contract with the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills"), after the Dolphins elected not to match the offer.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### Trades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Trades")]

An unusually large number of big name players switched teams via trade prior to the 2015 season.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Eagles coach Chip Kelly used his newly obtained GM powers to make many blockbuster trades. The [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/2015_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2015 Philadelphia Eagles season") traded 2-time All-Pro running back [LeSean McCoy](/wiki/LeSean_McCoy "LeSean McCoy") to the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/2015_Buffalo_Bills_season "2015 Buffalo Bills season") in exchange for linebacker [Kiko Alonso](/wiki/Kiko_Alonso "Kiko Alonso").[[39]](#cite_note-39) The Eagles also traded [Pro Bowl](/wiki/Pro_Bowl "Pro Bowl") quarterback [Nick Foles](/wiki/Nick_Foles "Nick Foles") along with their selection in the second round of the [2016 NFL draft](/wiki/2016_NFL_draft "2016 NFL draft") to the [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/2015_St._Louis_Rams_season "2015 St. Louis Rams season") for quarterback [Sam Bradford](/wiki/Sam_Bradford "Sam Bradford"); the deal also included a swap of draft picks in the [2015 NFL draft](/wiki/2015_NFL_draft "2015 NFL draft") as well as a possible additional 2016 draft pick from the Rams contingent on Bradford's playing time with the Eagles.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2015_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2015 New Orleans Saints season") traded All-Pro tight end [Jimmy Graham](/wiki/Jimmy_Graham "Jimmy Graham") along with their fourth-round selection in the [draft](/wiki/2015_NFL_draft "2015 NFL draft") to the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2015_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2015 Seattle Seahawks season") in exchange for All-Pro center [Max Unger](/wiki/Max_Unger "Max Unger") and the Seahawks' first-round selection in the [draft](/wiki/2015_NFL_draft "2015 NFL draft").[[41]](#cite_note-41) The Saints also traded away Pro Bowl guard [Ben Grubbs](/wiki/Ben_Grubbs "Ben Grubbs") (to the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/2015_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2015 Kansas City Chiefs season") for a fifth round selection in the [2015 NFL draft](/wiki/2015_NFL_draft "2015 NFL draft")) and wide receiver [Kenny Stills](/wiki/Kenny_Stills "Kenny Stills") (to the [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/2015_Miami_Dolphins_season "2015 Miami Dolphins season") in exchange for linebacker [Dannell Ellerbe](/wiki/Dannell_Ellerbe "Dannell Ellerbe") and a third rounder in the [2015 draft](/wiki/2015_NFL_draft "2015 NFL draft")). The [Detroit Lions](/wiki/2015_Detroit_Lions_season "2015 Detroit Lions season") acquired All-Pro defensive tackle [Haloti Ngata](/wiki/Haloti_Ngata "Haloti Ngata") from the [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/2015_Baltimore_Ravens_season "2015 Baltimore Ravens season") in exchange for draft picks to help make up for the loss of [Ndamukong Suh](/wiki/Ndamukong_Suh "Ndamukong Suh") in free agency.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

### Draft

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Draft")]

Further information: [2015 NFL draft](/wiki/2015_NFL_draft "2015 NFL draft")

The 2015 NFL draft was held April 30 – May 2, 2015, in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago").[[43]](#cite_note-43) The draft process began with the [NFL Scouting Combine](/wiki/NFL_Scouting_Combine "NFL Scouting Combine"), where draft-eligible players were evaluated by team personnel, which was held in [Indianapolis](/wiki/Indianapolis "Indianapolis") on February 17–23. On October 2, 2014, [Auditorium Theatre](/wiki/Auditorium_Theatre "Auditorium Theatre") in Chicago was announced as the official site of the draft.[[44]](#cite_note-Chicago-44) The previous fifty NFL drafts (since [1965](/wiki/1965_NFL_draft "1965 NFL draft")) had been held in New York.[[45]](#cite_note-NYDN_draft_venue-45) The 2015 NFL draft was the first to feature an outdoor component, where fans would be able to see the [Commissioner](/wiki/Roger_Goodell "Roger Goodell") on the Auditorium Theatre stage from across the street in [Grant Park](/wiki/Grant_Park_(Chicago) "Grant Park (Chicago)"); this area was called Draft Town.[[46]](#cite_note-46) In the draft, the [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/2015_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2015 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season") made [Florida State University](/wiki/2014_Florida_State_Seminoles_football_team "2014 Florida State Seminoles football team") quarterback [Jameis Winston](/wiki/Jameis_Winston "Jameis Winston") the [first overall selection](/wiki/List_of_first_overall_National_Football_League_draft_picks "List of first overall National Football League draft picks").[[47]](#cite_note-47)

## New officials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: New officials")]

[Referee](/wiki/List_of_National_Football_League_officials "List of National Football League officials") [Bill Leavy](/wiki/Bill_Leavy "Bill Leavy") retired after the [2014](/wiki/2014_NFL_season "2014 NFL season") season.[[48]](#cite_note-48) On May 13, 2015, the [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL") promoted line judge [John Hussey](/wiki/John_Hussey_(American_football_official) "John Hussey (American football official)") to the referee position.[[49]](#cite_note-49) In addition to Hussey's promotion to referee, the NFL hired 10 more officials, including the first full-time female official in NFL history, [Sarah Thomas](/wiki/Sarah_Thomas_(American_football_official) "Sarah Thomas (American football official)"), who worked as a line judge,[[note 1]](#cite_note-SarahThomas-50)[[50]](#cite_note-51) as well as Walt Coleman IV, the son of referee [Walt Coleman](/wiki/Walt_Coleman "Walt Coleman"), who worked as a side judge.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

## Rule changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Rule changes")]

The following rule changes were passed for the 2015 NFL season at the owners' meeting on March 25, 2015:[[52]](#cite_note-53)

* Allow a certified medical trainer to call a medical time-out when a player appears disoriented or concussed. This time-out is not to be charged to the team whose player is injured, even inside the [two-minute warning](/wiki/Two-minute_warning "Two-minute warning"). The only substitution allowed is for the injured player and for a single player from the opposing team to match up.
* Making the practice of [a receiver declared ineligible lining up in the](/wiki/A-11_offense "A-11 offense") [slot formation](/wiki/Slotback "Slotback") illegal; ineligible receivers must line up inside the [tackle box](/wiki/Tackle_box_(American_football) "Tackle box (American football)"). This was in response to the [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots") using this tactic in the [2014–15 NFL playoffs](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_NFL_playoffs#AFC:_New_England_Patriots_35,_Baltimore_Ravens_31 "2014–15 NFL playoffs") against the [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens").
* Extended the restriction for peel-back blocks to include all of the offensive team instead of just those inside of the tackle box.
* Expanded the definition of a "defenseless receiver" to include intended receivers in the air during and after an interception.
* Making offensive backs who chop a defender engaged above the waist by another offensive player subject to a chop-block penalty (15 yards).
* Pushing teammates at the scrimmage line during punts or field goals is illegal.
* Expanded instant replay to include whether time should be put back on the game clock at the end of any period.
* The [league's jersey numbering system](/wiki/National_Football_League_uniform_numbers "National Football League uniform numbers") was modified to allow linebackers wear numbers 40–49, in addition to 50–59 and 90–99.
* The 'process' rule on making a catch while going to the ground is adjusted. A receiver will be considered to have made a catch if they "clearly establish themselves as a runner" before going to the ground. Previously a receiver had to make a "football move".[[53]](#cite_note-54)

The following changes to the [extra point rules](/wiki/Conversion_(gridiron_football) "Conversion (gridiron football)") were passed for the 2015 NFL season at the owners' meeting on May 19, 2015:[[54]](#cite_note-55)[[55]](#cite_note-56)

* The line of scrimmage for extra point tries will move to the 15-yard line from the two-yard line. Two-point conversions will still be spotted at the two-yard line.
* Defenses will be allowed to return turnovers on two-point tries or blocked or missed PAT kicks to the opposing end zone for two points, mirroring the NCAA College football rule adopted in the [1988 season](/wiki/1988_NCAA_Division_I-A_football_season "1988 NCAA Division I-A football season"). Furthermore, a [one-point safety](/wiki/One-point_safety "One-point safety") can now also be scored if either team takes possession and fumbles out of their own end zone or is tackled in it after leaving it.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

The following changes to game ball protocol were passed for the 2015 NFL season on July 27, 2015:[[57]](#cite_note-58)

* There will be increased testing, oversight and security surrounding the balls. At random games, officials will measure the PSI of 24 footballs at halftime. Two officials, instead of only the referee, will measure and record the inflation of footballs before the game. These changes were made in response to the "[deflategate](/wiki/Deflategate "Deflategate")" scandal.

## 2015 deaths

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2015 deaths")]

The following people associated with the NFL (or [AFL](/wiki/American_Football_League "American Football League")) died in 2015.[[58]](#cite_note-PFR_2015_deaths-59)

Frank Gifford

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/4/48/FrankGifford.jpg)](/wiki/File:FrankGifford.jpg)

Gifford in 2009

[Frank Gifford](/wiki/Frank_Gifford "Frank Gifford") died on August 9, just a week shy of his 85th birthday. Gifford had a 12-year playing career with the [New York Giants](/wiki/History_of_the_New_York_Giants_(1925%E2%80%9378)#Jim_Lee_Howell_and_the_Hall_of_Famers:_1954-1958 "History of the New York Giants (1925–78)") in the 1950s and 1960s. He was a 4-time [All-Pro](/wiki/All-Pro "All-Pro"), played in eight [Pro Bowls](/wiki/Pro_Bowl "Pro Bowl") and was named to the [1950s All-Decade Team](/wiki/National_Football_League_1950s_All-Decade_Team "National Football League 1950s All-Decade Team"). In [1956](/wiki/1956_NFL_season "1956 NFL season") Gifford was named the NFL's [Most Valuable Player](/wiki/National_Football_League_Most_Valuable_Player_award "National Football League Most Valuable Player award"). After his playing career Gifford transitioned to sports broadcasting, most notably joining ABC's [Monday Night Football](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football") in the program's second season and serving as the broadcast's [play-by-play](/wiki/Sports_commentator#Main/Play-by-play_commentator "Sports commentator") announcer and later [color commentator](/wiki/Color_commentator "Color commentator") for the next 27 seasons. Gifford was a member of the [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") as well as the [College Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/College_Football_Hall_of_Fame "College Football Hall of Fame").[[59]](#cite_note-Gifford_NYT_obit-60)[[60]](#cite_note-Gifford_PFR-61)

St. Clair in 2009

Chuck Bednarik

[Chuck Bednarik](/wiki/Chuck_Bednarik "Chuck Bednarik") died on March 31 at age 89. Bednarik was the first player selected in the [1949 NFL draft](/wiki/1949_NFL_draft "1949 NFL draft") and played [linebacker](/wiki/Linebacker "Linebacker") and [center](/wiki/Center_(gridiron_football) "Center (gridiron football)") for the Philadelphia Eagles for fourteen seasons. He won championships with the Eagles in [1949](/wiki/1949_NFL_Championship_Game "1949 NFL Championship Game") and [1960](/wiki/1960_NFL_Championship_Game "1960 NFL Championship Game"). He was a 5-time first team [All-Pro](/wiki/All-Pro "All-Pro") and played in eight [Pro Bowls](/wiki/Pro_Bowl "Pro Bowl"). Bednarik was selected as a member of the [NFL's 75th Anniversary All-Time Two-Way Team](/wiki/National_Football_League_75th_Anniversary_All-Time_Team#75th_Anniversary_All-Time_Two_Way_Team "National Football League 75th Anniversary All-Time Team") and was inducted into the [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") in his first year of eligibility in 1967. He was also a member of the [College Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/College_Football_Hall_of_Fame "College Football Hall of Fame").[[61]](#cite_note-Bednarik_PhillyInq_obit-62)[[62]](#cite_note-Bednarik_PFR-63)

Ken Stabler

[Ken Stabler](/wiki/Ken_Stabler "Ken Stabler") died on July 8 at age 69. Stabler had a fifteen-year NFL career as a quarterback for the [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders#1972–1978 "Oakland Raiders"), [Houston Oilers](/wiki/History_of_the_Houston_Oilers#1980s "History of the Houston Oilers") and [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") in the 1970s and 1980s. He was named the league's [MVP](/wiki/National_Football_League_Most_Valuable_Player_award "National Football League Most Valuable Player award") in [1974](/wiki/1974_NFL_season "1974 NFL season"), led the Raiders to their first championship in [Super Bowl XI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XI "Super Bowl XI") and was a member of the [1970s All-Decade Team](/wiki/National_Football_League_1970s_All-Decade_Team "National Football League 1970s All-Decade Team").[[63]](#cite_note-Stabler_LATimes_obit-64)[[64]](#cite_note-Stabler_PFR-65)

Ed Sabol

[Ed Sabol](/wiki/Ed_Sabol "Ed Sabol") died on February 9 at age 98. Sabol founded [NFL Films](/wiki/NFL_Films "NFL Films") in 1962 where he pioneered a documentary style of capturing the game of football that many credit as a driver of the huge surge in the NFL's popularity. Sabol was enshrined in the [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") in 2011.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

Charlie Sanders

[Charlie Sanders](/wiki/Charlie_Sanders "Charlie Sanders") died on July 2 at age 68. Sanders played [tight end](/wiki/Tight_end "Tight end") for the [Detroit Lions](/wiki/Detroit_Lions "Detroit Lions") for ten seasons. He was twice named first-team [All-Pro](/wiki/All-Pro "All-Pro"), was selected for seven [Pro Bowl](/wiki/Pro_Bowl "Pro Bowl") teams and was a member of the [1970s All-Decade Team](/wiki/National_Football_League_1970s_All-Decade_Team "National Football League 1970s All-Decade Team"). After his playing career he continued to be involved with the Lions, first as a broadcaster and later as a coach and scout. He was elected to the [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") in 2007.[[66]](#cite_note-Sanders_DetroitFP_obit-67)[[67]](#cite_note-68)

Garo Yepremian

[Garo Yepremian](/wiki/Garo_Yepremian "Garo Yepremian") died on May 15 at age 70. Yepremian had a fourteen-year career as a [placekicker](/wiki/Placekicker "Placekicker") for four NFL teams, most notably with the Miami Dolphins of the 1970s. He was twice named a first-team [All-Pro](/wiki/All-Pro "All-Pro") and was a member of two [Pro Bowl](/wiki/Pro_Bowl "Pro Bowl") teams as well as a 2-time Super Bowl champion. He led the league in field goal accuracy three times and was named to the [1970s All-Decade Team](/wiki/National_Football_League_1970s_All-Decade_Team "National Football League 1970s All-Decade Team").[[68]](#cite_note-Yepremian_obit-69)[[69]](#cite_note-Yepremian_PFR-70)

Bob St. Clair

[Bob St. Clair](/wiki/Bob_St._Clair "Bob St. Clair") died on April 20 at age 84. St. Clair had an eleven-year career as an [offensive tackle](/wiki/Offensive_tackle "Offensive tackle") for the San Francisco 49ers in the 1950s and 1960s. He played in the [Pro Bowl](/wiki/Pro_Bowl "Pro Bowl") five times and was named to the [1950s All-Decade Team](/wiki/National_Football_League_1950s_All-Decade_Team "National Football League 1950s All-Decade Team"). St. Clair was the mayor of [Daly City, California](/wiki/Daly_City,_California "Daly City, California") from 1958 through 1964. St. Clair was enshrined in the [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") in 1990.[[70]](#cite_note-StClair_obit-71)[[71]](#cite_note-StClair_HOF_obit-72)[[72]](#cite_note-StClair_PFR-73)

## Preseason

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Preseason")]

[Training camps](/wiki/Training_camp_(National_Football_League) "Training camp (National Football League)") for the 2015 season began July 22 and continued through the end of preseason, September 3. The normal training camp window ran from late July to late August or early September. Most of the camps had rookies report first, then veterans. At that point, some teams practiced versus another organization, like the [Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills") practiced against the [Browns](/wiki/Cleveland_Browns "Cleveland Browns") this year. Teams started training camp no earlier than fifteen days before the team's first scheduled preseason game. At that point, the rosters for each team were open to 90 players. Those rosters were cut to 75 by Week 3 of preseason, and the final 53-man roster was submitted at the end of preseason.

Prior to the start of the regular season, each team played at least four [exhibition games](/wiki/Exhibition_game "Exhibition game"). The [preseason](/wiki/National_Football_League_preseason "National Football League preseason") schedule got underway with the [Pro Football Hall of Fame Game](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame_Game "Pro Football Hall of Fame Game") on Sunday evening, August 9. The Hall of Fame game is a traditional part of the annual [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") induction weekend celebrating new Hall of Fame members. It was played at [Tom Benson Hall of Fame Stadium](/wiki/Tom_Benson_Hall_of_Fame_Stadium "Tom Benson Hall of Fame Stadium") which is located adjacent to the Hall of Fame building in [Canton, Ohio](/wiki/Canton,_Ohio "Canton, Ohio"). The game, which was televised in the U.S. on [NBC](/wiki/NFL_on_NBC "NFL on NBC"), featured the [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings") and [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers"); as in previous years, each team had an inductee in the class of 2015 ([Mick Tingelhoff](/wiki/Mick_Tingelhoff "Mick Tingelhoff") for the Vikings, [Jerome Bettis](/wiki/Jerome_Bettis "Jerome Bettis") for the Steelers).[[73]](#cite_note-74) The 65-game preseason schedule ended on Thursday, September 3, a week before the start of the regular season, with each team having played four preseason games, except for the Steelers and Vikings, who played five games. The preliminary preseason schedule was released Thursday, April 9.

## Regular season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Regular season")]

The 2015 [regular season](/wiki/National_Football_League_regular_season "National Football League regular season") featured 256 games played out over a seventeen-week schedule which began on the Thursday night following [Labor Day](/wiki/Labor_Day "Labor Day"). Each of the league's 32 teams played a 16-game schedule with one [bye week](/wiki/Bye_(sports) "Bye (sports)") for each team scheduled between weeks four and eleven. The slate also featured seventeen games on [Monday night](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football"), two of which were played at the end of the first week of the regular season. Additionally, there was no Monday Night game at the end of the final week of the regular season, the same as in previous years. There were games played on Thursday, including the opening game of the regular season on Thursday, September 10 and three games on [Thanksgiving Day](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day"). The regular season concluded with a full slate of 16 games on Sunday, January 3, all of which were intra-divisional matchups, as it has been since [2010](/wiki/2010_NFL_season "2010 NFL season"), with the Minnesota Vikings beating the Green Bay Packers on *NBC Sunday Night Football*

Scheduling formula

Under the NFL's [scheduling formula](/wiki/NFL_scheduling_formula "NFL scheduling formula"), each team plays each of the other three teams in their own division twice (one home and one away). In addition, a team plays against all four teams in one other division within the conference, on a 3-year rotation; and one division from the opposite conference, on a 4-year rotation. Two games on a team's schedule are against the two teams in the team's own conference in the divisions the team was not set to play who finished the previous season in the same rank in their division (e.g. the team which finished first in its division the previous season would play each other team in their conference that also finished first in its respective division). The pre-set division pairings for 2015 are as follows:[[74]](#cite_note-75)

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Intra-conference**  [AFC East](/wiki/AFC_East "AFC East") vs. [AFC South](/wiki/AFC_South "AFC South")  [AFC North](/wiki/AFC_North "AFC North") vs. [AFC West](/wiki/AFC_West "AFC West")  [NFC East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East") vs. [NFC South](/wiki/NFC_South "NFC South")  [NFC North](/wiki/NFC_North "NFC North") vs. [NFC West](/wiki/NFC_West "NFC West") | **Inter-conference**  [AFC East](/wiki/AFC_East "AFC East") vs. [NFC East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East")  [AFC West](/wiki/AFC_West "AFC West") vs. [NFC North](/wiki/NFC_North "NFC North")  [AFC North](/wiki/AFC_North "AFC North") vs. [NFC West](/wiki/NFC_West "NFC West")  [AFC South](/wiki/AFC_South "AFC South") vs. [NFC South](/wiki/NFC_South "NFC South") |

Highlights of the 2015 schedule include:

* **[NFL Kickoff Game](/wiki/NFL_Kickoff_Game "NFL Kickoff Game")**: The 2015 season began on Thursday, September 10, 2015, with the [Super Bowl XLIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIX "Super Bowl XLIX") champion [New England](/wiki/2015_New_England_Patriots_season "2015 New England Patriots season") hosting [Pittsburgh](/wiki/2015_Pittsburgh_Steelers_season "2015 Pittsburgh Steelers season"). The Patriots defeated the Steelers, 28–21.
* **[International Series](/wiki/NFL_International_Series "NFL International Series")**: Three games were played at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") in [London, United Kingdom](/wiki/London,_United_Kingdom "London, United Kingdom") in 2015, with two games being played in back-to-back weeks for the first time.[[75]](#cite_note-IntlSeries-76) [Miami](/wiki/2015_Miami_Dolphins_season "2015 Miami Dolphins season") played the [New York Jets](/wiki/2015_New_York_Jets_season "2015 New York Jets season") on Sunday, October 4, the first ever divisional game played in the International Series.[[75]](#cite_note-IntlSeries-76) The [Jacksonville](/wiki/2015_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2015 Jacksonville Jaguars season") then hosted the [Buffalo](/wiki/2015_Buffalo_Bills_season "2015 Buffalo Bills season") on Sunday, October 25, in the third of four consecutive home games for the Jaguars in the International Series. Finally, the [Kansas City](/wiki/2015_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2015 Kansas City Chiefs season") hosted the [Detroit](/wiki/2015_Detroit_Lions_season "2015 Detroit Lions season") on Sunday, November 1,[[75]](#cite_note-IntlSeries-76) making 2015 the Lions' second consecutive year in the International Series. All three games began at 9:30 a.m. ET.[[76]](#cite_note-77)
* **[Thanksgiving Day games](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day")**: These games occurred on Thursday, November 26, 2015. For the second consecutive season, the [AFC](/wiki/American_Football_Conference "American Football Conference") was shut out of Thanksgiving, with all six slots going to NFC teams. As usual, three consecutive games were played; the [Detroit](/wiki/2015_Detroit_Lions_season "2015 Detroit Lions season") hosted the [Philadelphia](/wiki/2015_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2015 Philadelphia Eagles season") in the early slot on Fox and the [Dallas](/wiki/2015_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2015 Dallas Cowboys season") hosted the [Carolina](/wiki/2015_Carolina_Panthers_season "2015 Carolina Panthers season") in the middle slot on CBS; this was the Panthers' first ever Thanksgiving appearance. The [Green Bay](/wiki/2015_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2015 Green Bay Packers season") hosted the [Chicago](/wiki/2015_Chicago_Bears_season "2015 Chicago Bears season") in the primetime game, where the Packers [retired](/wiki/List_of_National_Football_League_retired_numbers "List of National Football League retired numbers") longtime quarterback [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre")'s No. 4 jersey.
* **[Christmas](/wiki/National_Football_League_Christmas_games "National Football League Christmas games")**: The [Oakland](/wiki/2015_Oakland_Raiders_season "2015 Oakland Raiders season") hosted the [San Diego](/wiki/2015_San_Diego_Chargers_season "2015 San Diego Chargers season") at 8:25 p.m. EST (5:25 p.m. local time) on December 24, Christmas Eve. This was the second ever night game on Christmas Eve (the first was in 2007, which was also played in the [Pacific Time Zone](/wiki/Pacific_Time_Zone "Pacific Time Zone")) and the first time the league has played on a Thursday Christmas Eve. The league has traditionally avoided playing night games on Christmas Eve and, in years past, moved games that would usually play on the night of Christmas Eve to another day of the week, an option the league did not exercise in 2015; no games were held on Christmas Day, which fell on a Friday, in 2015 as the NFL rarely plays games on that day of the week.

### In-season scheduling changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: In-season scheduling changes")]

* **Week 10:** The [Chicago](/wiki/2015_Chicago_Bears_season "2015 Chicago Bears season")–[St. Louis](/wiki/2015_St._Louis_Rams_season "2015 St. Louis Rams season") game was "cross-flexed" from [Fox](/wiki/Fox_NFL "Fox NFL") to [CBS](/wiki/NFL_on_CBS "NFL on CBS") (still at 1:00 p.m. [ET](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone")).[[77]](#cite_note-Week11Flex-78)
* **Week 11:** The [Cincinnati](/wiki/2015_Cincinnati_Bengals_season "2015 Cincinnati Bengals season")–[Arizona](/wiki/2015_Arizona_Cardinals_season "2015 Arizona Cardinals season") game, originally scheduled at 4:05 p.m. [ET](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone") on CBS, was flexed into the 8:30 p.m. ET slot on [NBC's Sunday Night Football](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football"), in place of the originally scheduled [Kansas City](/wiki/2015_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2015 Kansas City Chiefs season")–[San Diego](/wiki/2015_San_Diego_Chargers_season "2015 San Diego Chargers season") game, which was moved to 4:05 p.m. ET on CBS. The [Green Bay](/wiki/2015_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2015 Green Bay Packers season")–[Minnesota](/wiki/2015_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2015 Minnesota Vikings season") game, originally scheduled at 1:00 p.m. ET, was moved to 4:25 p.m. ET (still on Fox).[[77]](#cite_note-Week11Flex-78)
* **Week 13:** The [Carolina](/wiki/2015_Carolina_Panthers_season "2015 Carolina Panthers season")–[New Orleans](/wiki/2015_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2015 New Orleans Saints season") game, originally scheduled at 1:00 p.m. ET, was moved to 4:25 p.m. ET (still on Fox), while the Cincinnati–[Cleveland](/wiki/2015_Cleveland_Browns_season "2015 Cleveland Browns season") game was "cross-flexed" from CBS to Fox (still at 1:00 p.m. ET).[[78]](#cite_note-79)
* **Week 14:** The [New England](/wiki/2015_New_England_Patriots_season "2015 New England Patriots season")–[Houston](/wiki/2015_Houston_Texans_season "2015 Houston Texans season") game, originally scheduled at 1:00 p.m. ET on CBS, was flexed into the 8:30 p.m. ET slot on NBC's Sunday Night Football, in place of the originally scheduled [Seattle](/wiki/2015_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2015 Seattle Seahawks season")–[Baltimore](/wiki/2015_Baltimore_Ravens_season "2015 Baltimore Ravens season") game, which was moved to 1:00 p.m. ET on Fox.[[79]](#cite_note-80)
* **Week 15:** The Arizona–[Philadelphia](/wiki/2015_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2015 Philadelphia Eagles season") game, originally scheduled at 1:00 p.m. ET on Fox, was flexed into the 8:30 p.m. ET slot on NBC's Sunday Night Football, in place of the originally scheduled Cincinnati–[San Francisco](/wiki/2015_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2015 San Francisco 49ers season") game, which was moved to 4:25 p.m. ET on CBS, while the [Buffalo](/wiki/2015_Buffalo_Bills_season "2015 Buffalo Bills season")–[Washington](/wiki/2015_Washington_Redskins_season "2015 Washington Redskins season") game was "cross-flexed" from CBS to Fox (still at 1:00 p.m. ET).[[80]](#cite_note-81)
* **Week 16:** The [New York Giants](/wiki/2015_New_York_Giants_season "2015 New York Giants season")–Minnesota game, originally scheduled at 1:00 p.m. ET on Fox, was flexed into the 8:30 p.m. ET slot on NBC's Sunday Night Football, in place of the originally scheduled Pittsburgh–Baltimore game, which was moved to 1:00 p.m. ET on CBS, while the [Indianapolis](/wiki/2015_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2015 Indianapolis Colts season")–[Miami](/wiki/2015_Miami_Dolphins_season "2015 Miami Dolphins season") game was "cross-flexed" from CBS to Fox (still at 1:00 p.m. ET). In addition, the [Jacksonville](/wiki/2015_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2015 Jacksonville Jaguars season")-New Orleans game was moved from 1:00 p.m. ET to 4:05 p.m. ET (still on CBS).[[81]](#cite_note-82)
* **Week 17:**[[82]](#cite_note-83)
  + The Minnesota–Green Bay game, originally scheduled at 1:00 p.m. ET on Fox, was selected as the final 8:30 p.m. ET *NBC Sunday Night Football* game of the season, which decided the [NFC North](/wiki/NFC_North "NFC North") division champion.
  + The Baltimore–Cincinnati game, originally scheduled at 1:00 p.m. ET on CBS, was "cross-flexed" to Fox (keeping the same kickoff time).
  + The Tampa Bay–Carolina game, originally scheduled at 1:00 p.m. ET on Fox, was moved to 4:25 p.m. ET (still on Fox).
  + The Oakland–Kansas City game, originally scheduled at 1:00 p.m. ET on CBS, was "cross-flexed" to 4:25 p.m. ET on Fox.
  + The St. Louis–San Francisco game, originally scheduled at 4:25 p.m. ET on Fox, was "cross-flexed" to CBS (keeping the same kickoff time).

## Postseason

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Postseason")]

Main article: [2015–16 NFL playoffs](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_NFL_playoffs "2015–16 NFL playoffs")

The 2015 playoffs opened with the Wild Card playoff round on the weekend of Saturday, January 9 and Sunday, January 10, 2016, with the winner of each of the games visiting the top two seeded teams in each conference. The Divisional round games were then played on the weekend of Saturday, January 16 and Sunday, January 17, 2016. The Conference championships were held on Sunday, January 24, 2016, with the [AFC Championship Game](/wiki/AFC_Championship_Game "AFC Championship Game") and the [NFC Championship Game](/wiki/NFC_Championship_Game "NFC Championship Game").

The [2016 Pro Bowl](/wiki/2016_Pro_Bowl "2016 Pro Bowl") was held on January 31, 2016, at the [Aloha Stadium](/wiki/Aloha_Stadium "Aloha Stadium") in [Honolulu, Hawaii](/wiki/Honolulu,_Hawaii "Honolulu, Hawaii"). The game continued the "unconferenced/draft" format that was started in [2014](/wiki/2014_Pro_Bowl "2014 Pro Bowl"), with [Jerry Rice](/wiki/Jerry_Rice "Jerry Rice") and [Michael Irvin](/wiki/Michael_Irvin "Michael Irvin") serving as the alumni captains. Team Irvin defeated Team Rice 49–27.

[Super Bowl 50](/wiki/Super_Bowl_50 "Super Bowl 50") decided the 2015 NFL Champion and was played at [Levi's Stadium](/wiki/Levi%27s_Stadium "Levi's Stadium") in [Santa Clara, California](/wiki/Santa_Clara,_California "Santa Clara, California") on Sunday, February 7, 2016. Instead of naming it Super Bowl L with [Roman numerals](/wiki/Roman_numerals "Roman numerals") like in previous Super Bowls, this game was marketed with the [Arabic numeral](/wiki/Arabic_numeral "Arabic numeral") "50".[[2]](#cite_note-ESPN20140604-2) According to Jaime Weston, the league's vice president of brand and creative, the primary reason was that the league's graphic designers had difficulty designing a suitable, aesthetically pleasing logo with only the Roman numeral "L".[[2]](#cite_note-ESPN20140604-2)
