## Notable events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Notable events")]

Some NFL-related events that made headlines in 2015 include:

Aftermath of Deflategate scandal

Further information: [Deflategate](/wiki/Deflategate "Deflategate")

In May, after a lengthy investigation led by [Ted Wells](/wiki/Ted_Wells "Ted Wells"), the external investigator appointed by the NFL, the league levied its punishment against the Patriots in the so-called "Deflategate" scandal. The scandal stemmed from the discovery that several of the footballs used by the Patriots during the previous season's AFC championship game were not within the league's inflation guidelines. The Patriots were fined $1 million and stripped of their first-round selection in the [2016 NFL draft](/wiki/2016_NFL_draft "2016 NFL draft") and their fourth-round selection in the [2017 NFL draft](/wiki/2017_NFL_draft "2017 NFL draft"). Quarterback [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady"), who the league's report determined was likely aware of a scheme to manipulate inflation levels, was suspended for four games. Additionally, two locker room attendants employed by the Patriots were suspended indefinitely.[[83]](#cite_note-94)

While the team agreed not to appeal the fine and draft pick revocation, Brady appealed his suspension. League commissioner Roger Goodell heard the appeal and confirmed the sentence on July 28. Immediately upon the announcement of the appeal verdict, the league filed suit against the [NFL Players Association](/wiki/NFL_Players_Association "NFL Players Association") in civil court in an effort to gain a ruling upholding the punishment.[[84]](#cite_note-95) Judge [Richard M. Berman](/wiki/Richard_M._Berman "Richard M. Berman") pushed the two sides hard to reach a settlement, but when they were unable to he ruled for Brady and the union vacating the suspension imposed by the league. Although the league appealed Judge Berman's ruling, Brady's suspension was lifted clearing the way for him to play in the season opener.[[85]](#cite_note-96)[[86]](#cite_note-97)

The two Patriots employees, assistant equipment manager John Jastremski and officials locker room attendant Jim McNally, who had previously been suspended by the team were allowed by the league to be reinstated in September. As a condition for the lifting of the suspensions, neither employee is allowed to be involved with the handling or preparation of game balls.[[87]](#cite_note-98)

Surprising retirements of several players

A number of relatively young NFL players walked away from the game prior to the 2015 season. The [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/2015_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2015 San Francisco 49ers season") lost three potential starters as linebackers [Patrick Willis](/wiki/Patrick_Willis "Patrick Willis") and [Chris Borland](/wiki/Chris_Borland "Chris Borland") and offensive tackle [Anthony Davis](/wiki/Anthony_Davis_(offensive_tackle,_born_1989) "Anthony Davis (offensive tackle, born 1989)") all announced their retirements. Former [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/2014_Pittsburgh_Steelers_season "2014 Pittsburgh Steelers season") linebacker [Jason Worilds](/wiki/Jason_Worilds "Jason Worilds") and [Tennessee Titans](/wiki/2014_Tennessee_Titans_season "2014 Tennessee Titans season") quarterback [Jake Locker](/wiki/Jake_Locker "Jake Locker") also both retired despite having yet to reach the age of 30.

Willis, who was regarded as one of the best middle linebackers in the league, played in just six games in [2014](/wiki/2014_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2014 San Francisco 49ers season") as he dealt with a toe injury. He announced in March his decision to retire at age 30 due to the chronic nature of the foot injuries he had endured in his eight-year career.[[88]](#cite_note-99)

Borland led the 49ers with 107 tackles in his rookie season in 2014 despite starting just eight games after being selected in the third round of the [2014 draft](/wiki/2014_NFL_draft#Pick_77 "2014 NFL draft"). Borland cited concerns over the potential long-term impact to his brain health in continuing to play the game in announcing his retirement which he did in March just days after Willis' announcement.[[89]](#cite_note-100)

Davis missed four games in 2014 with his first diagnosed concussion. In announcing his retirement in June, he said that he planned to take "a year or so away from the NFL" to "allow my brain and body a chance to heal."[[90]](#cite_note-101)

Worilds was entering free agency after playing for the Steelers the previous five seasons. He turned down contract offers worth tens of millions of dollars in order to devote more time to his [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses") faith.[[91]](#cite_note-102)

Locker played four years for the Titans after they made him the eighth overall selection in [2011 draft](/wiki/2011_NFL_draft#Pick_8 "2011 NFL draft"). He was a free agent when he announced that he would be retiring because he had lost "the burning desire necessary to play the game for a living."[[92]](#cite_note-103)

First female coach and on-field official

The 2015 season marked the hiring of the first female NFL coach and first female NFL on-field official. [Jen Welter](/wiki/Jen_Welter "Jen Welter") was hired by the [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/2015_Arizona_Cardinals_season "2015 Arizona Cardinals season") as a coaching intern. Welter worked with the team's inside linebackers through the off-season and pre-season.[[93]](#cite_note-104) Welter's internship with the Cardinals expired after the team's third preseason game on August 30.[[94]](#cite_note-105)

[Sarah Thomas](/wiki/Sarah_Thomas_(American_football_official) "Sarah Thomas (American football official)") became the NFL's first female on-field official when she was hired by the league in April.[[note 1]](#cite_note-SarahThomas-50) Thomas had previously become the first female to officiate a major college football game as well as the first to officiate a [bowl game](/wiki/Bowl_game "Bowl game").[[96]](#cite_note-107)

Official suspended one game due to timekeeping gaffe

The NFL suspended side judge Rob Vernatchi (from [Pete Morelli](/wiki/Pete_Morelli "Pete Morelli")'s crew) for Week 6 of the regular season due to a timekeeping blunder that occurred during a game the previous week. The host [San Diego Chargers](/wiki/2015_San_Diego_Chargers_season "2015 San Diego Chargers season") kicked a field goal to take a 3-point lead over the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/2015_Pittsburgh_Steelers_season "2015 Pittsburgh Steelers season") with 2:56 remaining in the fourth quarter. The subsequent kickoff was a touchback, which shouldn't have resulted in any time coming off the game clock. However, when the Steelers took over on offense the scoreboard clock read 2:38. Vernatchi was responsible for keeping the official game time, but he did not notice the 18-second discrepancy. The Steelers ended up scoring a touchdown to secure a win with no time left on the clock.[[97]](#cite_note-108)

Referee crew demoted following questionable calls

The referee crew led by Pete Morelli had been assigned to officiate a *[Sunday Night Football](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football")* game between the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/2015_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2015 Indianapolis Colts season") and [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/2015_Pittsburgh_Steelers_season "2015 Pittsburgh Steelers season") in Week 13, but was reassigned to a different game ([Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/2015_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2015 Philadelphia Eagles season") at [New England Patriots](/wiki/2015_New_England_Patriots_season "2015 New England Patriots season")), due to heavy criticism for questionable calls made during the previous week's [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/2015_Arizona_Cardinals_season "2015 Arizona Cardinals season")–[San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/2015_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2015 San Francisco 49ers season") game. Morelli's crew drew the ire of Cardinals' head coach [Bruce Arians](/wiki/Bruce_Arians "Bruce Arians") and 49ers' safety [Eric Reed](/wiki/Eric_Reid_(American_football) "Eric Reid (American football)"), including a botched roughing-the-passer call on Cardinals' quarterback [Carson Palmer](/wiki/Carson_Palmer "Carson Palmer") that aided the Cardinals' eventual game-winning drive, as well as a missed delay-of-game penalty. Morelli's crew had previously been the subject of criticism, following a clock error during a Week 5 game – *see above*.[[98]](#cite_note-109) In addition, Morelli's crew was involved in a missed call at the end of the [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/2015_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2015 Jacksonville Jaguars season")–[Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/2015_Baltimore_Ravens_season "2015 Baltimore Ravens season") game in Week 10, where they missed a false start penalty against the Jaguars before the final snap that resulted in a facemask penalty against the Ravens when time expired, allowing the Jaguars to gain 15 yards and kick the game-winning field goal.[[99]](#cite_note-110)

### Discipline for off-field incidents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Discipline for off-field incidents")]

A total of 26 players were suspended by the league as of the season's first week. Most of these suspensions were for violations of the league's [performance-enhancing drug (PED)](/wiki/Performance-enhancing_drugs "Performance-enhancing drugs"), substance abuse and personal conduct policies.[[100]](#cite_note-111)

Browns GM suspended for texting scandal

[Cleveland Browns](/wiki/2015_Cleveland_Browns_season "2015 Cleveland Browns season") general manager [Ray Farmer](/wiki/Ray_Farmer "Ray Farmer") was suspended by the league for the first four games of the 2015 season due to a texting scandal which occurred in [2014](/wiki/2014_NFL_season "2014 NFL season"). The league found that Farmer had used a cellphone to communicate with personnel on the Browns' sideline "on multiple occasions during games" in violation of league rules which prohibit such communications. In addition to Farmer's suspension, the team was assessed a fine of $250,000.[[101]](#cite_note-112)

Falcons stripped of draft pick after being caught supplementing crowd noise

The league stripped the [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/2015_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2015 Atlanta Falcons season") of their selection in the fifth round of the [2016 draft](/wiki/2016_NFL_draft "2016 NFL draft") after it was determined that they had been using pre-recorded crowd noise during the team's home games throughout [2013](/wiki/2013_NFL_season "2013 NFL season") and into [2014](/wiki/2014_NFL_season "2014 NFL season"). In addition to losing the draft pick the franchise was fined $350,000 and team president [Rich McKay](/wiki/Rich_McKay "Rich McKay") was suspended from his post as chairman of the league's Competition Committee for three months starting in April. The team fired event marketing director Roddy White who they determined was directly responsible for the violation.[[102]](#cite_note-113)[[103]](#cite_note-114)

Bills suspend assistant coach Aaron Kromer after assault arrest

[Buffalo Bills](/wiki/2015_Buffalo_Bills_season "2015 Buffalo Bills season") offensive line coach [Aaron Kromer](/wiki/Aaron_Kromer "Aaron Kromer") was suspended by the team for the first six weeks of the season after he was arrested for an altercation during which Kromer allegedly punched a teenager. The incident occurred in July near Kromer's home in Florida. Charges in the matter were eventually dropped.[[104]](#cite_note-115)[[105]](#cite_note-116)[[106]](#cite_note-117)

Browns assistant coach Andy Moeller suspended following an alleged domestic incident

[Cleveland Browns](/wiki/2015_Cleveland_Browns_season "2015 Cleveland Browns season") offensive line coach [Andy Moeller](/wiki/Andy_Moeller "Andy Moeller") was suspended indefinitely by the Browns in September after in incident in which police were called to Moeller's house after a female houseguest alleged that Moeller physically assaulted her.[[107]](#cite_note-118) Prosecutors declined to press charges related to the incident despite their conclusion that "it is quite clear an incident of volatile nature took place."[[108]](#cite_note-119) On September 29, 2015, the Browns officially parted ways with Moeller.[[109]](#cite_note-120)

## Records, milestones, and notable statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Records, milestones, and notable statistics")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article **contains an excessive amount of intricate [detail](/wiki/Wikipedia:DETAIL "Wikipedia:DETAIL")**. Please help [improve it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit) by [spinning off](/wiki/Wikipedia:Content_forks#Article_spinoffs:_.22Summary_style.22_meta-articles_and_summary_sections "Wikipedia:Content forks") or [relocating](/wiki/Wikipedia:Handling_trivia#Recommendations_for_handling_trivia "Wikipedia:Handling trivia") relevant information and removing excessive detail that goes against [Wikipedia's inclusion policy](/wiki/Wikipedia:What_Wikipedia_is_not "Wikipedia:What Wikipedia is not"). *(December 2015)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Week 1

* [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") became the quarterback with the most regular season wins for a single franchise in NFL history ([161 victories](/wiki/List_of_most_wins_by_a_National_Football_League_starting_quarterback "List of most wins by a National Football League starting quarterback")), breaking the record held by [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre").[[110]](#cite_note-121)
* [Marcus Mariota](/wiki/Marcus_Mariota "Marcus Mariota") became the first quarterback in NFL history to gain a perfect 158.3 [passer rating](/wiki/Passer_rating "Passer rating") in his NFL debut and the first quarterback in NFL history to throw four TD passes in the first half of his NFL debut.[[111]](#cite_note-122) He also became the youngest quarterback to gain a perfect passer rating (21 years, 318 days) surpassing [Robert Griffin III](/wiki/Robert_Griffin_III "Robert Griffin III").[[112]](#cite_note-123)
* [Brandon McManus](/wiki/Brandon_McManus "Brandon McManus") became the second kicker in NFL history to make multiple field goals of 56 or more yards in the same game, joining [Greg Zuerlein](/wiki/Greg_Zuerlein "Greg Zuerlein"), who did it in [2012](/wiki/2012_NFL_season "2012 NFL season").[[113]](#cite_note-124)

Week 2

* [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") became the second quarterback in NFL history to reach 70,000 regular season passing yards, joining [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre").[[114]](#cite_note-125)
* The [Denver Broncos](/wiki/2015_Denver_Broncos_season "2015 Denver Broncos season") set a new NFL record for consecutive division away wins with 13, surpassing the previous record that the San Francisco 49ers set between 1987 and 1990.[[115]](#cite_note-126)
* The [New York Giants](/wiki/2015_New_York_Giants_season "2015 New York Giants season") became the first team in NFL history to start a season 0–2 while holding double digit leads in the fourth quarter in both games.[[116]](#cite_note-127)
* [Marcus Mariota](/wiki/Marcus_Mariota "Marcus Mariota") became the first quarterback in NFL history to throw six TD passes in his first two career games.[[117]](#cite_note-128)

Week 3

* [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") became the fourth quarterback in NFL history to throw [400 touchdown passes](/wiki/List_of_National_Football_League_career_passing_touchdowns_leaders "List of National Football League career passing touchdowns leaders") in his career, joining [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning"), [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre"), and [Dan Marino](/wiki/Dan_Marino "Dan Marino").[[118]](#cite_note-Career_Passing_Touchdowns-129)
* [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") became the second quarterback in NFL history to reach 6,000 pass completions, joining [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre").[[119]](#cite_note-Career_Passes_Completed-130)
* [Marcus Mariota](/wiki/Marcus_Mariota "Marcus Mariota") tied the NFL record set by [Mark Rypien](/wiki/Mark_Rypien "Mark Rypien") in 1988 with eight TD passes in his first three career games.[[120]](#cite_note-131)
* The [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/2015_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2015 Atlanta Falcons season") became the first team in NFL history to start a season 3–0 after trailing in the fourth quarter in all three games.[[121]](#cite_note-132)

Week 4

* [Drew Brees](/wiki/Drew_Brees "Drew Brees") became the fifth quarterback in NFL history to throw 400 touchdown passes and became also the third quarterback in NFL history to reach 5,000 pass completions in his career.[[118]](#cite_note-Career_Passing_Touchdowns-129)[[119]](#cite_note-Career_Passes_Completed-130)
* [Adam Vinatieri](/wiki/Adam_Vinatieri "Adam Vinatieri") became the first player in NFL history to score 1,000 points with two different teams.[[122]](#cite_note-133)

Week 5

* The [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/2015_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2015 Indianapolis Colts season") recorded an NFL record 16th consecutive division win.[[123]](#cite_note-134)
* [Matthew Stafford](/wiki/Matthew_Stafford "Matthew Stafford") and [Dan Orlovsky](/wiki/Dan_Orlovsky "Dan Orlovsky") combined to tie an NFL record for most pass attempts by one team in a game with 70 pass attempts during the Lions' loss to the [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/2015_Arizona_Cardinals_season "2015 Arizona Cardinals season").[[124]](#cite_note-135)
* [Devonta Freeman](/wiki/Devonta_Freeman "Devonta Freeman") became the first player in the Super Bowl era to rush for at least seven touchdowns in his first three starts to begin a career.[[125]](#cite_note-scout.com-136)
* [Antonio Gates](/wiki/Antonio_Gates "Antonio Gates") caught his 100th receiving touchdown and became the ninth player and second tight end to reach this mark in NFL history.[[126]](#cite_note-137)
* [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") surpassed Brett Favre as the NFL's all-time leader in combined regular season and postseason passing yards.[[127]](#cite_note-138)

Week 6

* [Aaron Rodgers](/wiki/Aaron_Rodgers "Aaron Rodgers") became the fastest quarterback in NFL history to throw for 30,000 yards, needing only 3,652 attempts to reach the mark.[[128]](#cite_note-139)

Week 7

* Five teams (the Panthers, Packers, Broncos, Bengals, and Patriots) started the season 6–0, setting a new NFL record for most teams to start a season 6–0 or better.[[129]](#cite_note-wk7-140)
* [Ryan Tannehill](/wiki/Ryan_Tannehill "Ryan Tannehill") set the NFL record for consecutive completed passes with 25, completing his first 18 passes his Week 7 game and the final seven of his previous game.[[130]](#cite_note-141)
* [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") became the fifth quarterback in NFL history to pass for over 55,000 yards.[[129]](#cite_note-wk7-140)

Week 8

* In a game between the [New York Giants](/wiki/2015_New_York_Giants_season "2015 New York Giants season") and the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2015_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2015 New Orleans Saints season"), the two teams combined for 13 touchdown passes, setting a new NFL record for the most combined passing touchdowns between both teams in a single game. The Saints defeated the Giants, 52–49. [Drew Brees](/wiki/Drew_Brees "Drew Brees") became the eighth quarterback to throw seven TD passes in a single game and [Eli Manning](/wiki/Eli_Manning "Eli Manning") became the first quarterback in NFL history to throw six TD passes with no interceptions but lose the game.[[131]](#cite_note-142) Brees also joined [Ben Roethlisberger](/wiki/Ben_Roethlisberger "Ben Roethlisberger") as one of only two quarterbacks in NFL history to throw for at least 500 yards in a game at least twice in a career.[[132]](#cite_note-143) The combined score of 101 points was, at the time, [the third highest scoring game in NFL history](/wiki/List_of_highest_scoring_NFL_games "List of highest scoring NFL games") (although it has since moved to the fourth highest scoring game). In addition, the Giants' 49 points tied the NFL record for the most points scored by a losing team (with the [Houston Oilers](/wiki/1963_Houston_Oilers_season "1963 Houston Oilers season")).
* [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") tied Brett Favre for the record of most regular season career victories all-time by a starting quarterback with the [Broncos](/wiki/2015_Denver_Broncos_season "2015 Denver Broncos season")' 29–10 win against the [Packers](/wiki/2015_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2015 Green Bay Packers season") with [186 wins](/wiki/List_of_most_wins_by_a_National_Football_League_starting_quarterback "List of most wins by a National Football League starting quarterback").[[133]](#cite_note-144)
* Four teams started the season 7–0, setting a new NFL record.[[134]](#cite_note-145)

Week 9

* For the first time in NFL history, three teams (the [Bengals](/wiki/2015_Cincinnati_Bengals_season "2015 Cincinnati Bengals season"), [Patriots](/wiki/2015_New_England_Patriots_season "2015 New England Patriots season"), and [Panthers](/wiki/2015_Carolina_Panthers_season "2015 Carolina Panthers season")) started the same season 8–0.[[135]](#cite_note-146)
* The [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/2015_Buffalo_Bills_season "2015 Buffalo Bills season") became the first team in NFL history with two 100-yard rushers ([LeSean McCoy](/wiki/LeSean_McCoy "LeSean McCoy") and [Karlos Williams](/wiki/Karlos_Williams "Karlos Williams")) and a 150-yard receiver ([Sammy Watkins](/wiki/Sammy_Watkins "Sammy Watkins")) in the same game.[[136]](#cite_note-147)
* [Marcus Mariota](/wiki/Marcus_Mariota "Marcus Mariota") became the first rookie quarterback in NFL history to have two games with four touchdowns and no interceptions.[[137]](#cite_note-148)

Week 10

* [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") became the NFL's all-time leader in [regular season passing yards](/wiki/List_of_National_Football_League_career_passing_yards_leaders "List of National Football League career passing yards leaders"), surpassing [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre").[[138]](#cite_note-149)
* For the first time in NFL history, six starting quarterbacks who had previously won a Super Bowl lost on the same day ([Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning"), [Eli Manning](/wiki/Eli_Manning "Eli Manning"), [Drew Brees](/wiki/Drew_Brees "Drew Brees"), [Aaron Rodgers](/wiki/Aaron_Rodgers "Aaron Rodgers"), [Joe Flacco](/wiki/Joe_Flacco "Joe Flacco"), and [Russell Wilson](/wiki/Russell_Wilson "Russell Wilson")).[[139]](#cite_note-150)
* [Adrian Peterson](/wiki/Adrian_Peterson "Adrian Peterson") tied [O. J. Simpson](/wiki/O._J._Simpson "O. J. Simpson") for an NFL record sixth career game with 200+ yards rushing.[[140]](#cite_note-151)
* The [New England Patriots](/wiki/2015_New_England_Patriots_season "2015 New England Patriots season") set a new NFL record by scoring in 35 consecutive quarters, breaking a record previously shared by the [1999 and 2000 St. Louis Rams](/wiki/2000_St._Louis_Rams_season "2000 St. Louis Rams season") and the [2005 Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/2005_Indianapolis_Colts "2005 Indianapolis Colts").[[141]](#cite_note-152)
* The [Detroit Lions](/wiki/2015_Detroit_Lions_season "2015 Detroit Lions season")' away win over the [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2015_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2015 Green Bay Packers season") snapped the Lions' 24-game losing streak at [Lambeau Field](/wiki/Lambeau_Field "Lambeau Field") (both regular season and postseason games), which was the longest away losing streak by one NFL team against another team in NFL history.[[142]](#cite_note-153)

Week 12

* [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") tied [Dan Marino](/wiki/Dan_Marino "Dan Marino") for third place on the all-time passing touchdowns list.[[143]](#cite_note-154)
* [Travis Coons](/wiki/Travis_Coons "Travis Coons") set an NFL record for most consecutive field goals made to open a career with 18.[[144]](#cite_note-155)

Week 13

* [Stephone Anthony](/wiki/Stephone_Anthony "Stephone Anthony") scored the first ever defensive two-point conversion in NFL history by returning a blocked point after touchdown attempt by [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/2015_Carolina_Panthers_season "2015 Carolina Panthers season") kicker [Graham Gano](/wiki/Graham_Gano "Graham Gano").[[145]](#cite_note-156)
* [Larry Fitzgerald](/wiki/Larry_Fitzgerald "Larry Fitzgerald") became the youngest person in NFL history to reach 1,000 career catches. Fitzgerald (32 years, 97 days old) broke the record that was held by [Andre Johnson](/wiki/Andre_Johnson "Andre Johnson") (33 years, 163 days).[[146]](#cite_note-157)
* [Andy Dalton](/wiki/Andy_Dalton "Andy Dalton") became the second quarterback in NFL history to throw for at least 3,000 passing yards in each of their first five seasons, joining [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning").[[147]](#cite_note-Quick_hits:_Green,_Dalton_in_select_company;_Rookies_ride-158)
* [A. J. Green](/wiki/A._J._Green "A. J. Green") joined [Randy Moss](/wiki/Randy_Moss "Randy Moss") as one of only two wide receivers in NFL history to have 1,000 yards in each of their first five seasons.[[147]](#cite_note-Quick_hits:_Green,_Dalton_in_select_company;_Rookies_ride-158)
* [Jason Witten](/wiki/Jason_Witten "Jason Witten") became the second tight end in NFL history to record 1,000 catches (joining [Tony Gonzalez](/wiki/Tony_Gonzalez "Tony Gonzalez")).[[148]](#cite_note-159)

Week 14

* [Adrian Peterson](/wiki/Adrian_Peterson "Adrian Peterson") scored his 100th career touchdown, making him the 13th running back to reach this mark in NFL history.[[149]](#cite_note-160)
* [Drew Brees](/wiki/Drew_Brees "Drew Brees") surpassed [Dan Marino](/wiki/Dan_Marino "Dan Marino") for fourth place on the NFL's all-time passing touchdowns list with two passing touchdowns.[[150]](#cite_note-Wk14SevenFromSunday-161)
* [Antonio Brown](/wiki/Antonio_Brown "Antonio Brown") became the sixth player in NFL history to record three straight 100-catch seasons, joining [Marvin Harrison](/wiki/Marvin_Harrison "Marvin Harrison"), [Jerry Rice](/wiki/Jerry_Rice "Jerry Rice"), [Herman Moore](/wiki/Herman_Moore "Herman Moore"), [Brandon Marshall](/wiki/Brandon_Marshall "Brandon Marshall"), and [Wes Welker](/wiki/Wes_Welker "Wes Welker").[[150]](#cite_note-Wk14SevenFromSunday-161)
* The [New England Patriots](/wiki/2015_New_England_Patriots_season "2015 New England Patriots season") clinched the [AFC East](/wiki/AFC_East "AFC East") division title for the seventh straight season, tying them with the [Rams franchise](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Rams "Los Angeles Rams"), which won seven straight [NFC West](/wiki/NFC_West "NFC West") division titles from the [1973 season](/wiki/1973_NFL_season "1973 NFL season") through the [1979 season](/wiki/1979_NFL_season "1979 NFL season"), for the NFL record of most consecutive division titles won by one franchise.[[151]](#cite_note-162)
* The [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers") became the second franchise in NFL history to record 750 total wins, including playoff victories, joining the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/Chicago_Bears "Chicago Bears") as the only teams to reach this mark.[[152]](#cite_note-Wk14WeekThatWas-163)
* [Matthew Stafford](/wiki/Matthew_Stafford "Matthew Stafford") became the fastest player to reach 25,000 yards as he reached this milestone in his 90th career game, which broke the record of 92 that was set by [Dan Marino](/wiki/Dan_Marino "Dan Marino").[[152]](#cite_note-Wk14WeekThatWas-163)
* [Russell Wilson](/wiki/Russell_Wilson "Russell Wilson") became the first quarterback in NFL history to have at least a 138.5 QB rating in four straight games.[[153]](#cite_note-164)

Week 15

* [Russell Wilson](/wiki/Russell_Wilson "Russell Wilson") became the first quarterback in NFL history to have at least three touchdown passes and no interceptions in five straight games.[[154]](#cite_note-165)
* [Cam Newton](/wiki/Cam_Newton "Cam Newton") became the first player in NFL history to rush for at least 100 yards and pass for at least 300 yards with five touchdowns in a single game.[[155]](#cite_note-Wk15ThatWas-166)
* [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") became the second quarterback in NFL history to throw at least 35 touchdown passes in a season four times, joining [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning").[[156]](#cite_note-167)
* [Drew Brees](/wiki/Drew_Brees "Drew Brees") became the fourth quarterback in NFL history to reach at least 60,000 regular season passing yards, joining Peyton Manning, Brett Favre, and Dan Marino. Brees became both the youngest and fastest quarterback to reach this mark in NFL history. Brees also set new NFL records for most consecutive seasons with at least 4,000 passing yards (ten straight seasons) and most regular season games with at least 300 passing yards in a career (94 games).[[155]](#cite_note-Wk15ThatWas-166)
* [Antonio Brown](/wiki/Antonio_Brown "Antonio Brown") set a new NFL record for the most receptions by any player in any three consecutive seasons in NFL history (355 receptions), breaking [Marvin Harrison](/wiki/Marvin_Harrison "Marvin Harrison")'s record of 354 receptions during the 2000–02 seasons.[[157]](#cite_note-168)
* The [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/2015_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2015 Kansas City Chiefs season") became the first team in NFL history to follow up a five-game losing streak with an eight-game winning streak.[[155]](#cite_note-Wk15ThatWas-166)

Week 16

* [Brandon Marshall](/wiki/Brandon_Marshall "Brandon Marshall") reached 100 catches for an NFL record sixth season. He also became the first Jets player to reach 100 catches in a season.[[158]](#cite_note-Wk16Seven-169)
* [Anquan Boldin](/wiki/Anquan_Boldin "Anquan Boldin") became the 13th player in NFL history to reach 1,000 career catches.[[158]](#cite_note-Wk16Seven-169)
* In their loss to the [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/2015_St._Louis_Rams_season "2015 St. Louis Rams season"), the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2015_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2015 Seattle Seahawks season") never gained a lead at any time, which ended their NFL record streak of 70 consecutive games (both regular season and playoff games) in which they had held a lead at any point during a game. This was the first time that the Seahawks failed to gain a lead at any point during a game since the final week of the 2011 season.[[159]](#cite_note-170)
* The [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/2015_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2015 Kansas City Chiefs season") became the second team since the [AFL-NFL merger](/wiki/AFL-NFL_merger "AFL-NFL merger") to clinch a playoff berth after going 1–5 over the first six games of a season, joining the [1970 Cincinnati Bengals](/wiki/1970_Cincinnati_Bengals "1970 Cincinnati Bengals").[[160]](#cite_note-171)
* [Cam Newton](/wiki/Cam_Newton "Cam Newton") became the first player in NFL history to throw at least 30 touchdown passes and score at least eight rushing touchdowns in a season.[[158]](#cite_note-Wk16Seven-169)
* [Antonio Brown](/wiki/Antonio_Brown "Antonio Brown") became the second player in NFL history to record 1,600 receiving yards in back to back seasons, joining [Calvin Johnson](/wiki/Calvin_Johnson "Calvin Johnson"), who accomplished the feat in 2011–12. Brown also became the second player in NFL history to record 120 catches in back to back seasons, joining [Cris Carter](/wiki/Cris_Carter "Cris Carter") (1994–95).[[161]](#cite_note-172)
* [Adam Vinatieri](/wiki/Adam_Vinatieri "Adam Vinatieri") became the third placekicker in NFL history to make 500 field goals in a career, joining [Morten Andersen](/wiki/Morten_Andersen "Morten Andersen") and [Gary Anderson](/wiki/Gary_Anderson_(placekicker) "Gary Anderson (placekicker)").[[162]](#cite_note-173)
* As of the end of week 16, the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2015_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2015 New Orleans Saints season") had surrendered 43 total passing touchdowns during the season, setting a new NFL record for most passing touchdowns surrendered during a season.[[163]](#cite_note-174)
* [Kirk Cousins](/wiki/Kirk_Cousins "Kirk Cousins") finished the season posting the highest completion percentage (74.7) in home games in NFL history, with a minimum of 100 attempts.[[164]](#cite_note-175)

Week 17

* [Russell Wilson](/wiki/Russell_Wilson "Russell Wilson") became the first quarterback in NFL history to have 4,000 or more passing yards, 30 or more passing touchdowns, and 500 or more rushing yards in the same season.[[165]](#cite_note-176)
* [Antonio Brown](/wiki/Antonio_Brown "Antonio Brown") set an NFL record of 265 receptions over the last two NFL seasons and became the first wide receiver in NFL history to record at least 1,700 yards from scrimmage in back-to-back seasons.[[166]](#cite_note-177)
* [Frank Gore](/wiki/Frank_Gore "Frank Gore") became the 15th player in NFL history to reach 12,000 career rushing yards.[[167]](#cite_note-178)
* [Drew Brees](/wiki/Drew_Brees "Drew Brees") finished the season with 4,870 passing yards, his NFL-record sixth consecutive season with at least 4,500 yards. Brees has seven career 4,500-yard passing seasons, the most in NFL history. Brees also led the league in passing for the sixth time in his career, setting an NFL record for most seasons leading the league, breaking the record of five that was shared by [Sonny Jurgensen](/wiki/Sonny_Jurgensen "Sonny Jurgensen") and [Dan Marino](/wiki/Dan_Marino "Dan Marino").[[168]](#cite_note-Wk17SevenSunday-179) In addition, Brees tied [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") for third most all-time regular season passing touchdowns after Brady failed to throw a TD pass during the [Patriots'](/wiki/2015_New_England_Patriots_season "2015 New England Patriots season") loss to the [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/2015_Miami_Dolphins_season "2015 Miami Dolphins season").[[169]](#cite_note-180)
* Eleven different quarterbacks passed for 30 or more touchdowns setting an NFL single season record. The previous record was nine quarterbacks set in 2014.[[170]](#cite_note-181)

Divisional Round

* [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") tied [Adam Vinatieri](/wiki/Adam_Vinatieri "Adam Vinatieri") for the most career postseason game appearances in NFL history (30).[[171]](#cite_note-182)
* [Brandon McManus](/wiki/Brandon_McManus "Brandon McManus") kicked five field goals in the Broncos' victory over the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/2015_Pittsburgh_Steelers_season "2015 Pittsburgh Steelers season"), tying the NFL record for most field goals made in a postseason game.[[172]](#cite_note-183)

Super Bowl 50

* The Denver Broncos became the fourth team to appear in eight Super Bowls, joining the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys"), [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots") and [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers").[[173]](#cite_note-184)
* [Denver Broncos](/wiki/2015_Denver_Broncos_season "2015 Denver Broncos season")' head coach [Gary Kubiak](/wiki/Gary_Kubiak "Gary Kubiak") became the first person in NFL history to reach a [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") as both a player and a head coach with the same franchise. Kubiak was [John Elway](/wiki/John_Elway "John Elway")'s backup quarterback on the Broncos from 1983 to 1991 and saw playing time in both [Super Bowl XXI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXI "Super Bowl XXI") and [Super Bowl XXIV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXIV "Super Bowl XXIV").[[174]](#cite_note-185) With [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/2015_Carolina_Panthers_season "2015 Carolina Panthers season")' head coach [Ron Rivera](/wiki/Ron_Rivera "Ron Rivera") having previously played as a linebacker for the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/Chicago_Bears "Chicago Bears") in [Super Bowl XX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XX "Super Bowl XX"), this game was the first ever Super Bowl in which both head coaches had formerly played in a Super Bowl themselves.[[175]](#cite_note-auto-186)
* This game was the first ever Super Bowl in which both starting quarterbacks were [No. 1 overall picks in an NFL draft](/wiki/List_of_first_overall_National_Football_League_draft_picks "List of first overall National Football League draft picks"); [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") was selected with the first overall pick in [1998](/wiki/1998_NFL_draft "1998 NFL draft") [Cam Newton](/wiki/Cam_Newton "Cam Newton") was selected with the first overall pick in [2011](/wiki/2011_NFL_draft "2011 NFL draft").[[175]](#cite_note-auto-186) This game was also the first Super Bowl to feature the top two overall picks from the same NFL draft on opposing teams; Newton and Broncos' linebacker [Von Miller](/wiki/Von_Miller "Von Miller") were selected first and second overall, respectively, in 2011.[[175]](#cite_note-auto-186)
* [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning"), at age 39, became the oldest quarterback to ever start a Super Bowl, surpassing John Elway who started [Super Bowl XXXIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXIII "Super Bowl XXXIII") at age 38.[[175]](#cite_note-auto-186) Manning also became the first quarterback in NFL history to lead two different teams to the Super Bowl multiple times;[[175]](#cite_note-auto-186) he previously led the Broncos to [Super Bowl XLVIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVIII "Super Bowl XLVIII") and also led the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts") to both [Super Bowl XLI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLI "Super Bowl XLI") and [Super Bowl XLIV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIV "Super Bowl XLIV"). With the Broncos' win, Manning surpassed Elway as the oldest quarterback ever to win a Super Bowl and also became the first quarterback in NFL history to lead two different teams to Super Bowl victories; he previously led the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts") to victory in [Super Bowl XLI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLI "Super Bowl XLI").
* [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") became the first quarterback in NFL history to record 200 combined regular season and postseason wins in a career. Manning surpassed [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre") (199) for the most combined regular season and postseason victories by a quarterback in a career.[[176]](#cite_note-187)

## Stadiums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Stadiums")]

Tennessee Titans' venue renamed Nissan Stadium

The stadium in [Nashville](/wiki/Nashville "Nashville") where the Tennessee Titans play their home games was renamed [Nissan Stadium](/wiki/Nissan_Stadium_(Nashville) "Nissan Stadium (Nashville)") in an agreement with automobile manufacturer [Nissan](/wiki/Nissan "Nissan"). Though financial terms remain undisclosed, the [naming rights](/wiki/Naming_rights "Naming rights") deal is expected to last for twenty years. Nissan operates two plants in [Tennessee](/wiki/Tennessee "Tennessee") and is one of middle Tennessee's largest employers. Since [2006](/wiki/2006_NFL_season "2006 NFL season"), the facility had been branded as "LP Field", under a naming rights agreement with Nashville-based [building materials](/wiki/Building_material "Building material") manufacturer [Louisiana-Pacific](/wiki/Louisiana-Pacific "Louisiana-Pacific"). This is the third name change for the venue since its [1999](/wiki/1999_NFL_season "1999 NFL season") opening.[[252]](#cite_note-263)

Minnesota Vikings last season at TCF Bank Stadium

The Minnesota Vikings played at [TCF Bank Stadium](/wiki/TCF_Bank_Stadium "TCF Bank Stadium") on the [University of Minnesota](/wiki/University_of_Minnesota "University of Minnesota") campus in [Minneapolis](/wiki/Minneapolis "Minneapolis") for the second straight season.[[253]](#cite_note-264) The Vikings arranged to play there for two years after their former home, the [Hubert H. Humphrey Metrodome](/wiki/Hubert_H._Humphrey_Metrodome "Hubert H. Humphrey Metrodome"), was closed after the [2013](/wiki/2013_NFL_season "2013 NFL season") season and demolished so [U.S. Bank Stadium](/wiki/U.S._Bank_Stadium "U.S. Bank Stadium"), their new stadium, could be built on the site.[[254]](#cite_note-265)
In August, a construction worker who was working on the new stadium died in a fall from the structure's roof.[[255]](#cite_note-266)

NRG Stadium Convert to Turf

[NRG Stadium](/wiki/NRG_Stadium "NRG Stadium"), home of the Houston Texans, converted from a natural grass field to an artificial turf field after week 1. Despite various attempts to improve field conditions, players from both the Texans and the visiting Kansas City Chiefs complained of poor field conditions in their week 1 game. The decision to convert was made 5 days later, and the turf field was installed in time for the Texans next home game against Tampa Bay in Week 3.[[256]](#cite_note-267)

### NFL relocation candidates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: NFL relocation candidates")]

#### Oakland Raiders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Oakland Raiders")]

See also: [History of the National Football League in Los Angeles](/wiki/History_of_the_National_Football_League_in_Los_Angeles "History of the National Football League in Los Angeles") and [Oakland Raiders relocation to Las Vegas](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders_relocation_to_Las_Vegas "Oakland Raiders relocation to Las Vegas")

The team's lease at the [Oakland Coliseum](/wiki/Oakland_Coliseum "Oakland Coliseum") expired after the 2013 season, and the Raiders after that point became tenants of the Coliseum on a year-to-year basis.[[257]](#cite_note-268)

##### San Antonio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: San Antonio")]

On July 29, 2014, reports surfaced that the Oakland Raiders might consider [relocating](/wiki/National_Football_League_franchise_moves_and_mergers "National Football League franchise moves and mergers") to [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio") in 2015 after owner [Mark Davis](/wiki/Mark_Davis_(American_football) "Mark Davis (American football)") met with San Antonio civic leaders the week before at the encouragement of former Raider [Cliff Branch](/wiki/Cliff_Branch "Cliff Branch"), whom Davis was in town to visit for a local ceremony for Branch. The Raiders themselves acknowledged Davis being in San Antonio for the event for Branch before news broke about a possible relocation, but would not confirm nor deny that Davis also mentioned being there discussing moving his team east.[[258]](#cite_note-Raiders-269) Among the two existing NFL teams in [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"), Houston Texans' then-owner [Bob McNair](/wiki/Bob_McNair "Bob McNair") and Dallas Cowboys' owner [Jerry Jones](/wiki/Jerry_Jones "Jerry Jones")—the latter of which has San Antonio as part of his territorial rights and previously voiced support of an NFL team moving there when the New Orleans Saints [temporarily played in San Antonio in 2005](/wiki/Effect_of_Hurricane_Katrina_on_the_New_Orleans_Saints "Effect of Hurricane Katrina on the New Orleans Saints") due to [damages to the Superdome following Hurricane Katrina](/wiki/Effect_of_Hurricane_Katrina_on_the_Louisiana_Superdome "Effect of Hurricane Katrina on the Louisiana Superdome") – both favored an NFL team playing in San Antonio.[[259]](#cite_note-270)

On September 3, 2014, the city of [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland "Oakland") reached a tentative deal to build a new football stadium in Oakland, which would have resulted in the Coliseum being demolished; Davis did not respond to the proposal, which would have also forced the [Oakland Athletics](/wiki/Oakland_Athletics "Oakland Athletics") to build a new stadium of their own (which they did not agree to do), while [Alameda County](/wiki/Alameda_County "Alameda County") (then co-owners of the coliseum) indicated they would probably not have supported the plan.[[260]](#cite_note-San_Francisco_Chronicle-271) Davis, in the meantime, had team officials scout the Alamodome to determine if it would be suitable for the NFL.[[261]](#cite_note-272)

##### Shared stadium with the Chargers in Los Angeles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Shared stadium with the Chargers in Los Angeles")]

Main article: [Carson Stadium](/wiki/Carson_Stadium "Carson Stadium")

On February 19, 2015, the Oakland Raiders and San Diego Chargers announced plans for a privately financed $1.7 billion [stadium](/wiki/Carson_Stadium "Carson Stadium") that the two teams would build in [Carson, California](/wiki/Carson,_California "Carson, California"), if they were to move to the [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") market.[[262]](#cite_note-273) Such a move would have marked a return to the nation's second-largest market for both teams; the Raiders played in Los Angeles from 1982 to 1994 while the Chargers called LA home for their inaugural season in the [American Football League](/wiki/American_Football_League "American Football League"). The Chargers were at the time the only NFL team to play in [Southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California"), with [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego") being a 125-mile (201 km) distance from Los Angeles and had Los Angeles as a secondary market. The Chargers had been looking to replace [Qualcomm Stadium](/wiki/Qualcomm_Stadium "Qualcomm Stadium") (which, like the O.co Coliseum opened in the late 1960s) since [at least 2003](/wiki/San_Diego_Chargers_stadium_proposals "San Diego Chargers stadium proposals"), and had an annual out clause in which it could move in exchange for paying a fine to the city of San Diego for its remaining years on its lease.

Due to television contracts, NFL bylaws, and being in the same division if both of the [longstanding division rivals](/wiki/Chargers%E2%80%93Raiders_rivalry "Chargers–Raiders rivalry") moved to Los Angeles, one of the teams would have had to move to the [National Football Conference](/wiki/National_Football_Conference "National Football Conference") and the [NFC West](/wiki/NFC_West "NFC West"), something that Mark Davis volunteered the Raiders to be willing to do. The Raiders' move to the National Football Conference and the NFC West would have been considered ironic because Davis's father [Al Davis](/wiki/Al_Davis "Al Davis") was a staunch opponent of the NFL during its rivalry and eventual [merger](/wiki/AFL%E2%80%93NFL_merger "AFL–NFL merger") with the AFL. If such a realignment had occurred, one of the existing NFC West teams would have taken their spot in the [AFC West](/wiki/AFC_West "AFC West"). The early rumor was that the Seattle Seahawks, who played in the AFC West from 1977 to 2001, would have been the favorite to switch conferences with the Raiders. However, that team's [then growing rivalry](/wiki/49ers%E2%80%93Seahawks_rivalry "49ers–Seahawks rivalry") with the San Francisco 49ers (who are assured of staying in the NFC West) pointed to either the Arizona Cardinals or the St. Louis Rams (if the latter had stayed in [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis")) moving to the AFC West to take the Raiders' spot. If the Rams stayed in St. Louis, switching them to the AFC would have allowed for a yearly home-and-home with the cross-state Kansas City Chiefs.[[263]](#cite_note-274)

On October 23, 2015, Chargers spokesperson Mark Fabiani confirmed that the team planned to officially notify the NFL about its intentions to relocate to Los Angeles in January during the timetable for when teams can request to relocate.[[264]](#cite_note-275)

#### St. Louis Rams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: St. Louis Rams")]

Main article: [SoFi Stadium](/wiki/SoFi_Stadium "SoFi Stadium")

The Rams and the St. Louis CVC (Convention & Visitors Commission) began negotiating deals to get the Rams' home stadium, the [Edward Jones Dome](/wiki/Edward_Jones_Dome "Edward Jones Dome"), into the top 25 percent of stadiums in the league (i.e., top eight teams of the thirty-two NFL teams in reference to luxury boxes, amenities and overall fan experience). Under the terms of the lease agreement, the St. Louis CVC was required to make modifications to the Edward Jones Dome in 2005. However, then-owner [Georgia Frontiere](/wiki/Georgia_Frontiere "Georgia Frontiere") waived the provision in exchange for cash that served as a penalty for the city's noncompliance. The City of [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), in subsequent years, made changes to the scoreboard and increased the natural lighting by replacing panels with windows, although the overall feel remained dark. The minor renovations which totaled about $70 million did not bring the stadium within the specifications required under the lease agreement.
On February 1, 2013, an arbitrator (3 panel) selected to preside over the arbitration process found that the Edward Jones Dome was not in the top 25% of all NFL venues as required under the terms of the lease agreement between the Rams and the CVC. The arbitrator further found that the estimated $700 million in proposed renovations by the Rams was not unreasonable given the terms of the lease agreement. Finally, the City of St. Louis was Ordered to pay the Rams attorneys' fees which totaled a reported $2 million.

Publicly, city, county and state officials expressed no interest in providing further funding to the Edward Jones Dome in light of those entities, as well as taxpayers, continuing to owe approximately $300 million more on that facility. As such, if a resolution was not reached by the end of the 2014–15 NFL season and the City of St. Louis remained non-compliant in its obligations under the lease agreement, the Rams were free to nullify their lease and relocate.

On January 31, 2014, both the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* and the *[St. Louis Post-Dispatch](/wiki/St._Louis_Post-Dispatch "St. Louis Post-Dispatch")* reported that Rams owner [Stan Kroenke](/wiki/Stan_Kroenke "Stan Kroenke") had purchased 60 acres of land adjacent to the [Forum](/wiki/The_Forum_(Inglewood) "The Forum (Inglewood)") in [Inglewood](/wiki/Inglewood,_California "Inglewood, California"), [Los Angeles County](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County "Los Angeles County"), [California](/wiki/California "California"). It was, by the most conservative estimates, sufficient land on which an NFL-proper stadium could be constructed. The purchase price was rumored to have been US$90–100 million. Commissioner Roger Goodell represented that Kroenke informed the league of the purchase. As an NFL owner, any purchase of land in which a potential stadium could be built must be disclosed to the league. This development further fueled rumors that the Rams intended to return its management and football operations to Southern California. The land was initially targeted for a Walmart Supercenter but [Walmart](/wiki/Walmart "Walmart") could not get the necessary permits to build the center. Kroenke is married to [Ann Walton Kroenke](/wiki/Ann_Walton_Kroenke "Ann Walton Kroenke"), who is a member of the [Walton family](/wiki/Walton_family "Walton family") and many of Kroenke's real estate deals have involved Walmart properties.[[265]](#cite_note-276)[[266]](#cite_note-277)[[267]](#cite_note-278) On January 5, 2015, *The Los Angeles Times* reported that [Kroenke Sports & Entertainment](/wiki/Kroenke_Sports_%26_Entertainment "Kroenke Sports & Entertainment") and [Stockbridge Capital Group](/wiki/Stockbridge_Capital_Group "Stockbridge Capital Group") were partnering to develop a new NFL stadium owned by Kroenke. The project included a stadium of up to 80,000 seats and a performance venue of 6,000 seats while reconfiguring the previously approved Hollywood Park plan for up to 890,000 square feet of retail, 780,000 square feet of office space, 2,500 new residential units, a 300-room hotel and 25 acres of public parks, playgrounds, open space and pedestrian and bicycle access. In lieu of this the city of St. Louis responded on January 9, 2015, by [unveiling an outdoor, open-air, riverfront stadium](/wiki/National_Car_Rental_Field "National Car Rental Field") that could accommodate the Rams and an [MLS](/wiki/MLS "MLS") team with the hopes that the NFL bylaws would force them to stay. On February 24, 2015, the Inglewood City Council approved the stadium and the initiative with construction on the stadium beginning in December 2015.

With the Chargers, Raiders and Rams proposing their own stadiums as part of their Los Angeles relocation contingency plans, the proposed [Farmers Field](/wiki/Farmers_Field_(Los_Angeles) "Farmers Field (Los Angeles)") project was permanently scrapped in March 2015.[[268]](#cite_note-shutdown-279) Farmers Field was a proposal from [Anschutz Entertainment Group](/wiki/Anschutz_Entertainment_Group "Anschutz Entertainment Group") to lure an NFL team to Los Angeles by promising a new stadium, but AEG placed restrictions on any relocation that the rest of the league found unacceptable, and the project had lain dormant since 2012.[[269]](#cite_note-280)

## Super Bowl 50 promotion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Super Bowl 50 promotion")]

To mark the [50th](/wiki/Golden_jubilee "Golden jubilee") [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl_50 "Super Bowl 50"), various gold-themed promotions and initiatives were held during the 2015 season, including gold-tinted logos across all NFL properties, the numbering of the 50-yard line on fields being painted in gold, sideline jackets and hats featuring gold-trimmed logos from week 7 onward, and [Pro Bowl](/wiki/2016_Pro_Bowl "2016 Pro Bowl") jersey designs incorporating gold numbering. Gold footballs were given to each high school that has had a player or coach appear in the Super Bowl, and "homecoming" events were held by teams at games.[[270]](#cite_note-wpost-nflgold15-281)[[1]](#cite_note-cbs-goldnfl2015-1)

Through their first two home games, the [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/2015_Oakland_Raiders_season "2015 Oakland Raiders season") declined to participate in the use of gold paint to mark the 50-yard line. On September 22, *[Sports Business Daily](/wiki/Sports_Business_Daily "Sports Business Daily")* reported that NFL VP/Communications Brian McCarthy stated it was because the [Oakland Athletics](/wiki/Oakland_Athletics "Oakland Athletics") were still playing at the [O.co Coliseum](/wiki/O.co_Coliseum "O.co Coliseum") (the only stadium that is still shared by both an NFL team and a [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") team, which forces the Raiders to play on its dirt infield until the baseball season concludes), but the Coliseum's General Manager Chris Wright responded by saying that the Raiders told him not to apply the gold marks for the remaining regular season games.[[271]](#cite_note-282) One day later, Raiders owner [Mark Davis](/wiki/Mark_Davis_(American_football) "Mark Davis (American football)") confirmed that the gold markings would be used after the conclusion of the Athletics season, boasting that because they had appeared in five of them, "nobody respects the Super Bowl more than Raiders".[[272]](#cite_note-283)

## New uniforms and patches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: New uniforms and patches")]

After it had been leaked months before by the Packers that both teams were allowed to wear their colored jerseys for *[Thursday Night Football](/wiki/Thursday_Night_Football "Thursday Night Football")* contests,[[273]](#cite_note-284) on October 30, 2015, the NFL announced "[Color Rush](/wiki/Color_Rush "Color Rush")", a series of four Thursday contests in which all eight teams will wear one-time, specially designed and monochromatic [alternate uniforms](/wiki/Third_jersey "Third jersey").[[274]](#cite_note-285) The Carolina Panthers and Tennessee Titans wore their regular alternate uniforms (with the Panthers debuting "Carolina blue" pants), while the Dallas Cowboys revived their white "Double Star" uniforms from the mid-1990s (while debuting white pants) and the St. Louis Rams wore a gold version of their 1973–99 throwbacks for the games. The other four teams involved (Buffalo Bills, Jacksonville Jaguars, New York Jets, and Tampa Bay Buccaneers) wore all-new uniforms for the games. The November 12 game between the Bills and Jets was particularly problematic, with the Bills' all-red uniforms (the first time the team has ever worn a red jersey on the field) and the Jets' kelly green outfits being indistinguishable to those with [color blindness](/wiki/Color_blindness "Color blindness").[[275]](#cite_note-286)

* The [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/2015_Cleveland_Browns_season "2015 Cleveland Browns season") unveiled new uniforms on April 14, 2015. There are three jersey colors and three pants colors: orange, brown, and white, allowing for nine possible uniform combinations. The uniforms have "Cleveland" above the numbers on the front, "Browns" down the leg, and "Dawg Pound" inside the collar. The uniforms are the first in the NFL to utilize contrast stitching and chainmail/raised numbers. The city name in front and team name down the leg are also NFL firsts.[[276]](#cite_note-287) On February 24, the Browns slightly tweaked their logo. The orange on their helmet is brighter and the facemask, which was gray, is now brown. The team also updated their secondary logo.[[277]](#cite_note-288)
* The [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/2015_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2015 San Francisco 49ers season") unveiled a new black alternate uniform on May 1, 2015. The jerseys have red numerals with matching black pants. This is the first alternate uniform in the team's history.[[278]](#cite_note-289) In addition, the 49ers sported a helmet decal and lapel pin honoring Hall of Fame offensive lineman [Bob St. Clair](/wiki/Bob_St._Clair "Bob St. Clair") who died in April; the decal had St. Clair's number 79 in white inside a black football shape.[[279]](#cite_note-NFL_2015_Kickoff_guide-290)
* The [Tennessee Titans](/wiki/2015_Tennessee_Titans_season "2015 Tennessee Titans season") switched their primary colored jerseys from light "Titans Blue" to navy blue, the latter of which was the team's primary home jersey color from 1999 to 2007.[[280]](#cite_note-291)
* The [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2015_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2015 Green Bay Packers season") unveiled a new [throwback uniform](/wiki/Throwback_uniform "Throwback uniform") on July 28, 2015. The design features the same layout the Packers used between 1937 and 1949, and again in 1994. Like the previous 1920s-inspired throwbacks, the base colors will be brown, navy blue and yellow.[[281]](#cite_note-292)
* The [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/2015_Miami_Dolphins_season "2015 Miami Dolphins season") unveiled a new [throwback uniform](/wiki/Throwback_uniform "Throwback uniform") to commemorate the team's 50th season on July 29, 2015. The uniform is a throwback to the Dolphins' inaugural season in 1966. The team also wore a patch celebrating the anniversary during the entirety of the season.[[279]](#cite_note-NFL_2015_Kickoff_guide-290)[[282]](#cite_note-293)
* The [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/2015_Baltimore_Ravens_season "2015 Baltimore Ravens season") wore a patch to commemorate the team's 20th season.[[279]](#cite_note-NFL_2015_Kickoff_guide-290)[[283]](#cite_note-294) On December 20, 2015, the team also unexpectedly debuted gold pants for the first time, wearing them with their regular purple jerseys against the Kansas City Chiefs. Although gold is an official accent color of the Ravens, the pants got an overwhelmingly negative response on [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media") by both Ravens fans and fans of other NFL teams, with some comparisons being made to the rival Pittsburgh Steelers pants.[[284]](#cite_note-295)
* The [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/2015_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2015 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season") wore a patch to commemorate the team's 40th season.[[279]](#cite_note-NFL_2015_Kickoff_guide-290)[[285]](#cite_note-296)
* The [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/2015_Oakland_Raiders_season "2015 Oakland Raiders season") upgraded their uniforms to incorporate [Nike](/wiki/Nike,_Inc. "Nike, Inc.")'s "Elite 51" technology, which most teams had been using since 2012.[[286]](#cite_note-297)
* The [New England Patriots](/wiki/2015_New_England_Patriots_season "2015 New England Patriots season") changed the logo on their jerseys from their traditional cursive wordmark to the serif block lettering they adopted when they updated their logo in 2013.[[287]](#cite_note-298)
* The [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/2015_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2015 Philadelphia Eagles season") wore a helmet decal in honor of Hall of Fame center/linebacker [Chuck Bednarik](/wiki/Chuck_Bednarik "Chuck Bednarik") who died in March. The decal featured Bednarik's number 60 in white inside a black circle.[[279]](#cite_note-NFL_2015_Kickoff_guide-290)
* The [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/2015_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2015 Atlanta Falcons season") wore a patch recognizing the team's 50th season.[[279]](#cite_note-NFL_2015_Kickoff_guide-290)
* The [New York Giants](/wiki/2015_New_York_Giants_season "2015 New York Giants season") honored the late [Frank Gifford](/wiki/Frank_Gifford "Frank Gifford") and [Ann Mara](/wiki/Ann_Mara "Ann Mara") by adding a number 16 helmet decal and patch for Gifford and Mara respectively.[[288]](#cite_note-299)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2015_NFL_season&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Media")]

This was the second season under the league's broadcast contracts with its television partners. On May 12, 2015, it was announced that [ABC](/wiki/ESPN_on_ABC "ESPN on ABC") would simulcast ESPN's wildcard game, marking the first time that ABC broadcast any NFL game in nearly 10 years.[[289]](#cite_note-wp-espnabcwildcard-300) ESPN also continued to carry *[Monday Night Football](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football")* and the [Pro Bowl](/wiki/Pro_Bowl "Pro Bowl"). [NBC](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football") continued to air *Sunday Night Football*, the annual [Kickoff game](/wiki/National_Football_League_Kickoff_game "National Football League Kickoff game"), and the primetime Thanksgiving game. This was also the second year that included "cross-flexing" (switching) Sunday afternoon games between [CBS](/wiki/NFL_on_CBS "NFL on CBS") and [Fox](/wiki/NFL_on_Fox "NFL on Fox") before or during the season, regardless of whether the visiting team is in the AFC (which CBS normally airs) or the NFC (which is normally carried by Fox).

After its successful inaugural season under the arrangement, the NFL extended CBS's contract for *[Thursday Night Football](/wiki/Thursday_Night_Football "Thursday Night Football")* into the 2015 season; as with the previous season, CBS produced all games, and the first seven games (weeks 2–7), as well as week 13, were broadcast by the CBS network. All games, including those not aired by CBS, were broadcast by [NFL Network](/wiki/NFL_Network "NFL Network").[[290]](#cite_note-thr-tnfpayday-301) The package also included one Saturday game in Week 15 and one in Week 16, both exclusive to NFL Network

Under a new eight-year deal, [DirecTV](/wiki/DirecTV "DirecTV") continued to be the exclusive distributor of the [NFL Sunday Ticket](/wiki/NFL_Sunday_Ticket "NFL Sunday Ticket") service.[[291]](#cite_note-302)[[292]](#cite_note-303)

Fox expanded its pre-game coverage by moving *[Fox NFL Kickoff](/wiki/Fox_NFL_Kickoff "Fox NFL Kickoff")* to the main Fox network from [Fox Sports 1](/wiki/Fox_Sports_1 "Fox Sports 1"), serving as a lead-in to *[Fox NFL Sunday](/wiki/Fox_NFL_Sunday "Fox NFL Sunday")*.[[293]](#cite_note-sbd-movetofox-304)

On March 23, 2015, league owners voted to, as an experiment, suspend the NFL's [blackout rules](/wiki/National_Football_League_television_blackout_policies "National Football League television blackout policies") for the 2015 season; no games were blacked out in their home markets because of insufficient ticket sales. These moves came after the [Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission")'s September 2014 decision to stop enforcing blackout rules on terrestrial television stations, and the fact that, ultimately, no games were blacked out at all during the 2014 season.[[294]](#cite_note-espn-nflblackoutoneyear-305)

The NFL also experimented with online [streaming](/wiki/Streaming "Streaming") as part of the International Series game between the Jacksonville Jaguars and Buffalo Bills. The game was streamed worldwide by [Yahoo!](/wiki/Yahoo! "Yahoo!"), who handled hosting, promotion, and advertising sales for the stream, while CBS produced the telecast.[[295]](#cite_note-verge-yahoonfl-306) The game only aired on television in the team's home markets (in accordance with NFL policies), as well as in the United Kingdom on [BBC Two](/wiki/BBC_Two "BBC Two") and [Sky Sports](/wiki/Sky_Sports "Sky Sports"), and in [China](/wiki/China "China").[[296]](#cite_note-307) [Brian Rolapp](/wiki/Brian_Rolapp "Brian Rolapp"), the league's executive vice president of media, explained that the experiment was part of the NFL's efforts to attempt alternative distribution models for games, such as those that would appeal to [viewers who do not subscribe to pay television](/wiki/Cord-cutting "Cord-cutting").[[295]](#cite_note-verge-yahoonfl-306)[[297]](#cite_note-nyt-billsonline-308) Yahoo! was reported to have paid $15 million for the rights fees, plus an additional $2 million "marketing fee", and beat out [Amazon.com](/wiki/Amazon.com "Amazon.com"), [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter"), and [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube")—some of whom had made higher bids but would have planned to place the broadcast behind a [paywall](/wiki/Paywall "Paywall"), which the league resisted.[[298]](#cite_note-309)

The NFL entered into a [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media") partnership with [Snapchat](/wiki/Snapchat "Snapchat") to present [live stories](/wiki/Snapchat#Stories_and_Discover "Snapchat") with behind-the-scenes and fan perspectives from selected games.[[299]](#cite_note-recode-nfllivestories-310)
